variants.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
variants.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.

variants.claim: Building a written character/persona for each creator and aligning all content to it raises LTV by enabling a parasocial relationship rather than generic thirst traps.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media sits each new model down to create an in-depth character sheet (occupation, personality, persona, backstory) and aligns outfits, scenes, and PPVs to it, deliberately targeting spending demographics by the model's look so chatters can monetize a fleshed-out character.

sources.quote: most of OFM doesn't know what brand actually means or confuses the concept of brand with niche
brand and only fans boils down to three questions. The first question is who is your audience? The second question is what is your identity? The third and final question is what is your story?
Stop posting for a second and tell me who the model actually is
figuring out her identity such as her vibe her audience her niche and then yeah just what makes her different
make it that her persona
you're actually a character with a story and depth... build a better parasocial relationship with them

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator brand framework (§2 GAP and §1 partial). The content pipeline (§1) is strong on viral ideation but does not appear to systematically define creator identity, target audience persona, or narrative/fantasy positioning before deciding what content to film. This is a meaningful strategic gap in how Palm directs creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a structured brand-definition step upstream of the content pipeline (§1). Before a creator enters the inspo/filming cycle, document: (1) their specific target audience segment, (2) their unique identity differentiators vs. comparable creators, (3) the core fantasy/emotional narrative they sell. Store this in the Creators table in Airtable and use it as a filter when promoting inspo reels to creators in Step 5 of the pipeline.

findings.title: Score every chatter on 5 KPIs to build a leaderboard before restructuring

variants.claim: Pulling 30 days of chat logs and scoring each chatter on response time, PPV buy rate, average fan spend, retention, and objection handling creates an objective leaderboard that drives all restructuring decisions.
variants.what_they_do: Pull last 30 days of chat logs for every chatter. Score each individual across: response time, PPV buy rate, average fan spend, retention rate, objection handling quality. Aggregate into a single leaderboard score per chatter. Use that ranked list as the sole basis for warnings, fires, and promotions.

variants.claim: Chatters underperforming is typically caught too late because there is no proactive weekly tracking system — by the time revenue drops, the damage is already done.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan embeds chat performance as one of the seven required pieces of a delivery operating system: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator compliance, creator complaints. The chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality as their primary number. Weekly scorecard review catches a declining chatter before the month closes. He contrasts reactive delivery (everyone panics after revenue drops) with proactive delivery (problem spotted before it becomes painful via data and tracking).

sources.quote: pull the last 30 days of chat logs for every single chatter and score them individually
you now have a list of all your chatters with an overall score. You now have a leaderboard
you spot the chatter that's performance is declining before the month closes out and the revenue drops
A chatter misses standards and nothing happens. That's the culture.
the chatter start to underperform. Someone notices too late cuz the chat manager slacked off

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources all chatting to an external team (§8) and has no internal chatter KPI framework or leaderboard. This audit methodology is exactly the kind of build-toward intelligence needed when Palm moves to an in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, implement this 5-KPI audit from day one. Define the scoring rubric before hiring chatter #1 so the leaderboard is operational at scale. Consider requiring the external chat team to report these same KPIs now as a baseline.

findings.title: Sell content as an escalating PPV script ladder

variants.claim: Monetize via a scripted sequence where each stage is more explicit and priced higher than the last, rather than flat pricing.
variants.what_they_do: A 'script' is pre-made content starting safe-for-work and escalating; price rises each stage, e.g. ~$10-15, then ~$35, ~$70, ~$140, ~$200. Between paid stages, send free photos at the level the fan already unlocked to over-deliver and stand out from other creators.

variants.claim: A pre-built escalation script that guides the fan through progressively more explicit content at increasing price points generates $300–$500 per subscriber per session vs. a one-off $50 custom video.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters use a fixed script structure: (1) open with small talk / lifestyle warmup, (2) send 1–2 free teaser images to raise arousal, (3) transition to selling with a tiered video ladder — e.g., $15 (tease only) → $30 (slightly more) → $60 (light action) → $120 (more explicit) → $200 (full action). Content explicitness scales with price. Subscriber buys each step sequentially; by the final video, the session is complete and they've spent $300–$500. The same script is reused across all subscribers.

variants.claim: Sending a free teaser photo followed by a scripted sequence of PPV content with escalating price points ($15, $50, $100, $150, $200) converts fans into high spenders by building investment and desire incrementally.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters follow a pre-built content flow the creator has shot in advance. Conversation starts organically, then a free clothed photo is sent. First PPV is priced low ($15–20, ~15–20 second video). Free content interspersed between paid unlocks to maintain engagement. Each subsequent PPV increases in price and explicitness. A single fan session can yield $300–400. Chatters run 24/7 in 8-hour rotating shifts (3 chatters per creator account), sourced primarily from South Africa for native English and cultural fluency.

sources.quote: the first one is fairly cheap, like $10, $15, then it goes to about 35, then maybe 70, 140, and then 200.
you can send a bunch of pictures for free... it's going to seem like you're providing so much more value.
you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200... you just continuously progress
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber and that's how your channel goes to the big big levels
the price goes from $15 to 50, then to 100, then to $150, then to $200, and before you know it, the person has spent maybe $300, $400

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP) and is thin on OF-vault/PPV content (§2); chatting is outsourced (§8) so the sequence is build-toward for in-house/AI chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture a default PPV script ladder (stage prices + free-content-between-stages tactic) as a starting pricing position and as build-toward content for the AI-chat project.

findings.title: Run a structured onboarding call covering 6 key areas before going live

variants.claim: A thorough onboarding call is the single most important trust-building moment and should cover mindset, content expectations, timeline, invoicing, credentials, and Q&A.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a dedicated onboarding call and systematically cover: (1) model's mindset and expectations, (2) content volume needed upfront and why, (3) realistic 3-month results timeline, (4) invoicing schedule explanation, (5) credential handover for socials and OF, (6) open Q&A floor for the model. They treat the call as a formal first impression, not a casual chat.

variants.claim: A formal onboarding call presentation — covering process steps, revision limits, invoice terms, cancellation policy, and 'how to win' — eliminates scope creep and buyer remorse by setting hard expectations before any work begins.
variants.what_they_do: Build a Canva slide deck covering: welcome/team intro, reiterate buying decision with case studies, step-by-step delivery process with timelines, revision policy (2 revisions, batch feedback in one email), invoicing terms, meeting cancellation policy (72hr notice), additional services pricing, and monthly strategy call cadence. Present this at the kickoff call before any strategy discussion. Use it as a live walk-through shared on screen. Embed the deck in the client's Notion client OS for future reference.

sources.quote: schedule that initial onboarding call and run it with the model this is that first impression
you need to establish some sort of a timeline and expectations as to when they should be able to receive the results
any frustration is just an unmet expectation. And if we're not doing a good job at setting expectations, then the client's going to be frustrated
I like to use Canva to build this out. So, if I'm on the client call... we're just going to go over a quick onboarding presentation just to make sure we're really aligned on expectations
revisions. If you do have revisions, super happy to help. We limit those to two

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (name, email, commission %, contract, voice memo) per §6 — it is async and document-driven. There is no documented structured live onboarding call. The form captures admin data but does not walk the creator through expectations, timelines, or content strategy.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a mandatory video onboarding call as Step 2 after the contract wizard. Build a call checklist (Notion/Airtable template) covering the 6 areas above. Record it for reference. This directly addresses the creator-trust gap implied by §6's thin onboarding and supports retention (§9).

findings.topic: Onboarding sequence — OF page setup before social access
findings.title: Structured 7-day onboarding: OF page + vault first (days 1–4), social access + content plan second (days 5–7)

variants.claim: A sequenced onboarding where the OF page is optimized and chatting infrastructure is ready before social accounts are touched reduces errors and accelerates time-to-revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Days 1–2: get OF login and Inflow access, optimize vault and OF page. Days 3–4: bio, subscription price, page optimization. Days 5–7: gain IG/TikTok/Twitter access, set up content distribution, scripts, chatting setup, and build content plan with creator. Content plan uses the 3-competitor research process (top videos → style selection → post → teach creator what to film and why).

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

variants.claim: Explicitly set what will happen in week 1, month 1, and month 2 so the creator doesn't form unrealistic expectations and churn.
variants.what_they_do: On the onboarding call, walk through the timeline for the first week and first two months. Rationale: 'if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own,' which are usually unrealistic and lead to early departure when results don't match.

sources.quote: Log in to the only fans in the inflow account. Optimize the vault, optimize the OF page. Then on days like seven, I want you to get into the Instagram, Tik Tok, and Twitter accounts
content strategy and create a content plan with her. So on that, I want you to do niche competitor research
you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.
if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own expectations, which typically is not really reasonable... they leave.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding form captures credentials and contract (§6) but there is no documented day-by-day onboarding sequence or ordering logic. The OF vault/PPV system is thin (§2) and no OF page optimization checklist exists. The social setup is mature for AI accounts (§4) but not formalized for real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a real-creator onboarding SOP mirroring the AI warmup cadence structure (§4): Days 1–4 = OF access + page/vault optimization checklist; Days 5–7 = social access + content plan creation. Add this as a checklist workflow in Airtable under the Creators table, triggered on creator status change to 'onboarding.'

findings.topic: Outreach message framing — authority + personalization, no direct pitch
findings.title: Open with a conversion-problem hook; never pitch the agency in the first DM

variants.claim: The first DM should establish authority by identifying a specific problem in the creator's setup rather than pitching the agency, and should funnel toward a no-pressure discovery call rather than explaining the revenue split.
variants.what_they_do: Template opener: 'Hey [Name], I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions.' Then a brief authority statement. Never mention the split in DMs — save that for the call. On the call: ask current earnings, show what's possible, then close on next steps and send a contract via Dropbox.

variants.claim: The opening DM should be short, reference one specific problem visible on the creator's profile (e.g., wrong link-in-bio, no reels), and pique curiosity — never a long copy-paste paragraph.
variants.what_they_do: Agents identify a concrete, observable weakness on the creator's profile before messaging, then write a brief DM that names that problem implicitly or asks a curiosity-driving question. No feature-dumping or price-quoting in the first message. Outreach is sent from a personal IG account even with as few as 150 followers.

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

sources.quote: Hey Name, I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions
Don't explain the split on the DMs. Obviously, explain the split on the actual call itself
focus on the problem that specific model has. So this could be the lack of posting reels, the wrong link in bio
I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach message template or sales-call funnel (§6 GAP). The split is set per-creator at onboarding but no pre-call qualifying script exists (§7 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Draft a 2–3 message DM sequence template: (1) problem-hook opener with authority, (2) 24-hr follow-up voice note, (3) call-to-action to book a 10–15 min discovery call. Create a call script with qualifying questions (current earnings, goals) before revealing the split. Store both in the acquisition runbook.

findings.title: Source creators across IG, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok, and prioritize referrals

variants.claim: Find creators by searching niche keywords across multiple platforms, but treat satisfied-client referrals as the highest-quality channel.
variants.what_they_do: Search 'only fans' / niche terms (e.g. gamer girl) on Instagram, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok and filter for link-in-bio + recent activity. TikTok surfaces up-and-comers; Reddit is where whales and creators already are. Best channel = referrals: after a great first month in the green, ask the happy creator who else does OnlyFans.

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

variants.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
variants.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.

sources.quote: first one of course is Instagram... Your other option is going to be Twitter... The next one is going to be Reddit... You can also find them on Tik Tok.
by far the best is referrals... after you've delivered a great service... ask, do you know of anybody else who's also doing Only Fans.
within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away
you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already uses referrals (§6) but has no documented multi-platform sourcing process or a systematic referral ask.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-platform sourcing process and bake a structured referral ask into the post-first-month creator-success cadence.

findings.topic: Client wins database as retention and sales ammo
findings.title: Maintain a running 'wins database' — screenshots, metrics, clips — per client for retention and resigning

variants.claim: Systematically capturing and storing client wins (lead screenshots, view counts, clips) in a tagged database gives the CSM live ammunition to counter 'are you doing anything for me?' objections, improves retention at renewal, and provides case study material for new sales.
variants.what_they_do: Notion 'wins' database with fields: win type (leads/views/revenue/video performance), client relation, screenshot or media URL, date. Anyone on the team can add a win. KPI: minimum X wins per client per month set as a CSM metric. Wins roll up to client OS so the CSM can open a client record and instantly see a count and list of wins. Used on retention calls, renewals, and sales calls. Goal: ~20+ wins/month company-wide as a tracked number on the client success dashboard.

variants.claim: Agencies should run a standing mechanism that captures every creator win, screenshot, and testimonial as it happens, since proof fuels both acquisition and trust.
variants.what_they_do: Routes every positive creator message, analytics screenshot, and voice note into a Drive/table immediately, so each win can later be turned into an ad or case study; recommends installing this even before running ads.

sources.quote: we want to create a culture at our company that we collect wins, we celebrate wins, and we stack wins. We have almost a thousand wins of just screenshots
I would try to get like at least a couple of wins a month for each client and make that like your KPI internally from like a client success perspective
when you're on a call with a client and they're like, 'Oh, I'm just not really seeing anything.' It's like, 'Dude, like we've gotten you 10 leads.'
start installing the systems to capture proof like immediately
you need to like start having a system within your agency that captures proof

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker tools (§9) for fan-level data, but no documented system for capturing and storing creator wins (viral posts, subscriber milestones, revenue records) as a retention tool. The §9 gap also notes no written retention strategy. A wins database is directly applicable: Palm's editors and social team observe creator wins daily and could log them systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Wins' table in Airtable (Content Ops or HQ base) with fields: creator, win type (milestone sub count, viral reel, PPV revenue record, press mention), media URL, date. Make logging a win a required step for editors/social team when they observe a notable metric. Josh or a creator success role reviews and shares wins back to creators weekly — this doubles as creator retention messaging and provides case study content for acquisition.

findings.title: Two-funnel X system: paid retweet drops plus comment-baiting

variants.claim: X/Twitter is run as a deliberate dual funnel combining paid retweet-group 'drops' for reach and manual niche comment-baiting for targeted conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Funnel 1: pay a retweet-group manager to drop one image/day for 100-500 retweets; Funnel 2: build a comment bank plus 30-image SFW bank, follow ~1k niche accounts (warmed slowly, 5-10/hr), comment 50-100/day with short CTA captions under target posts; track via Fanvue tracking links targeting 3-5% click-to-sub CTR.

variants.claim: X growth is driven by reply-baiting engagement traps, fast trendjacking, and paying large promo pages to seed content with mystery hooks.
variants.what_they_do: Posts reply-bait ('describe me in one word'), jumps trends like Grok/Epstein image gimmicks within 15-30 min, and pays promo pages (100-500k followers) $50-200/post that hook viewers into clicking through to the creator's pinned reply.

variants.claim: Replying to competing creators' posts with a relevant matching photo of your model hijacks their engagement and drives profile visits and follows at zero cost.
variants.what_they_do: Identify 10–20 OF creator accounts in the same niche on X. When those accounts post (e.g., bikini beach pic), the burner account replies with a relevant matching photo of the managed creator and a natural caption (e.g., 'What about my bikini at the beach?'). Also retweet competitor posts to appear in front of their audience. Comment on any post going viral in the broader niche to farm free impressions. VA hired to execute this spam engagement loop throughout the day.

sources.quote: I call it the twofunnel system
A good CTR is 3 to 5%
the content designed to get replies
Reply to that with relevant things. What about my bikini at the beach? And then attach a photo of you at your model at a beach. What this is going to do is hijack their engagement.
You guys can hire a VA to do this. I have my VAs to do this. Just make sure your spam engagement.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Traffic/distribution strategy beyond Publer/Telegram posting is an explicit gap (§5/§8); X is not in Palm's documented funnel.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot an X comment-baiting plus tracking-link funnel for real creators as a low-cost channel and document CTR benchmarks; comment-bait mechanics transfer to real creators.

findings.title: Run a multi-platform traffic mix; no single source carries it

variants.claim: Reliable revenue requires combining several traffic sources rather than relying on one, with each platform graded on traffic quality, not raw views.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends Instagram (primary, highest-quality subs), Threads, and X together, with TikTok optional; X drives high volume but lower-quality subs; explicitly moves away from pure rage-bait because '28.5M views' does not equal money — quality and niche of traffic matter for conversion.

variants.claim: No single platform is the permanent best traffic source — TikTok and Instagram Reels alternate in effectiveness over weeks, while Reddit bot services offer more stable, updatable automation.
variants.what_they_do: Monitors which platform is currently converting and shifts effort accordingly (e.g., moved from TikTok to IG Reels during a two-month TikTok slump, then back). Reddit bot/automation services are treated as a stable baseline because they can be updated when Reddit changes its algorithm or removes bots. Maintains awareness of Reddit API/bot policy changes.

variants.claim: New agencies should limit traffic sources to organic short-form content on Twitter, TikTok, and Instagram, deferring Reddit, bots, and shoutouts until the funnel is proven.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends a three-platform organic-only traffic strategy for beginners: push short-form video content on Twitter/X, TikTok, and Instagram. Explicitly deprioritizes Reddit, paid shoutouts, and automation tools as advanced tactics — not because they don't work, but because complexity at the start obscures what's actually driving results.

sources.quote: you need to be using a combination of all different traffic sources
it doesn't always mean views equals money

there is no best it always varies Tick Tock for the last two weeks it is working but the last two months before that it was very very like it just genuinely it was just bad so Instagram reels were better so it always changes
things that work really really well at least for us that we found are like bot Services they don't really mess up and it's pretty easy to then update these bot Services as well
start simple things like Twitter, Tik Tok, Instagram, and that's it. Just push organic short form content. No need to over complicate it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is Publer + Telegram-to-Amin posting (§5 PARTIAL) with traffic strategy across channels undocumented (§5 gap).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a documented multi-channel traffic mix per creator with per-channel quality grading, so channel investment is steered by conversion quality not view counts.

findings.title: Main + 2-4 Burner Account Structure to Grow X Presence Fast

variants.claim: Running one premium 'main' account supported by 2–4 high-volume burner accounts is the core engine for rapid X follower growth and OF revenue.
variants.what_they_do: One main account is treated as the premium asset — high quality content only, no full nudity, goal of 50–100K impressions per post. Two to four burner accounts run parallel at higher volume (3–5 posts/day vs 1–3 on main), used for trend testing, engagement farming, and follower accumulation. Once burners hit 1–5K followers, all traffic is funneled to the main via pinned tweets, bio links, and reply CTAs. Main account holds the OF link; burners never monetize directly.

variants.claim: On X, a protected main brand account funneled by several disposable high-volume burner accounts maximizes growth while insulating the asset from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Runs one main account (2-3 quality posts/day, funnels to OF) plus 3-5 burner accounts (3-4 aggressive trend posts/day) that spam trends and pin replies funneling all traffic to the main; if a burner is banned, it's disposable.

variants.claim: Running hundreds of warmed-up Instagram accounts via cloud phones on automated schedules generates 50–200 additional subs per day per creator while insulating the main account from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency spins up 200–300 Instagram accounts on cloud-hosted real phones. Automations handle: feed scrolling, liking, Reel posting, phone-number verification — all to mimic organic human behavior and avoid suspensions. None of these satellite accounts carry the OF link in bio; they funnel only to the creator's main IG page (or to 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives only on the main page. Suspension rate drops to ~1-in-20 vs. higher rates when the OF link is in satellite bios. If main account is banned, warmed satellite accounts are handed to the creator to restart on.

sources.quote: You're going to have two to four accounts in this depending on where you're at. The account one is going to be your main account.
These are your growth engine
we set up hundreds of Instagram accounts... you can run everything off the cloud
In none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages
if we have 200 phones for one creator, and we're literally pulling one subscriber per day from each of the phones, then that's 200 subs a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is a documented GAP (§5). Palm has no X/Twitter presence or strategy documented. This is a fully absent channel for Palm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 1-main + 2-burner X account structure for each real creator. Assign a VA to run burner engagement (spam commenting, trend monitoring, retweet loop) daily. Main account managed by editor/ops. Start with one creator as a test before rolling out.

findings.title: Never link OF/Fanvue directly; warm accounts before linking

variants.claim: To avoid bans, operators never post adult-platform links directly on social and warm new accounts before adding any link-in-bio CTA.
variants.what_they_do: Always route via a neutral landing page (e.g. Get All My Links) since a reported direct OF/Fanvue link can trigger strikes/bans; spend the first ~30 days on profile setup, spaced posting and trust-score building before putting links in bio; avoid rapid-burst follows/comments and automation that trip platform limits.

variants.claim: New social accounts should be warmed as normal users and post content for about two weeks before adding the OnlyFans link to protect trust score.
variants.what_they_do: Acts as a normal user (scroll/like, follow niche on day 2, don't post day 1), waits ~2 weeks of posting before adding any link, and pre-builds accounts before signing a client so revenue never stalls.

variants.claim: New social accounts are warmed for about a week of human-like activity and niche engagement before any adult link is added, to build trust score and avoid early bans.
variants.what_they_do: Day-by-day: matching Gmail/name, scroll+like niche reels to train the algorithm, neutral (non-model) profile pic, follow ~15 niche creators/day, engage and like older US/UK 'simp' commenters under competitor posts to pull them over (never DM), then post first reel ~day 7 and only add the link.bio funnel 2-4 weeks in when followers justify the risk.

sources.quote: never post direct to Fan View. Always use the Get All My Links
warm up slowly, act like a human
post content for 2 weeks before adding a link
how you set up and warm up an Instagram properly within 7 days to avoid being banned
Build up your Instagram account slowly and patiently

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature with a 90-day cadence, Day-21 link handoff and Day-45 OF-CTA gate (§4), but real-creator warmup and link-safety rules are a gap (§4 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a real-creator account-warmup plus landing-page-only link policy mirroring the AI warmup gates (§4) to reduce strike/ban risk on creator socials.

findings.title: An AI chatbot can replace much of the chat team at a fraction of the cost

variants.claim: A well-configured AI chatting bot can handle 24/7 fan conversations and PPV upsells at a fraction of a chat team's cost, but only if it is carefully set up and trained.
variants.what_they_do: Uses Super Creator's 'Izzy' AI chatbot (~$200-300/month vs a full chatting team), configured with extensive persona data, slang, pricing strategy, and trained on rapport/texting/selling scenarios; warns that a bad setup just creates 'a shitty chatter'.

variants.claim: AI chatbots are improving rapidly and will soon deliver low-latency, high-quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest human VAs, making human chatting teams economically unviable.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant flags AI chat as an imminent market force that will undercut human chatters (including cheap Filipino VA teams) on both cost and latency, framing it as a structural shift rather than a distant future trend.

variants.claim: The emerging chatting meta is a hybrid where AI handles new-subscriber volume and identifies real spenders, then hands those fans to human chatters for long-term girlfriend-experience retention.
variants.what_they_do: Has AI run all new subscribers and complete the first script to separate spenders from time-wasters, then moves real spenders to a human chatter who gives a daily girlfriend experience to a small client list (~20 fans each); reports ~70% unlock rate and a single whale spending $50k in 4 months.

sources.quote: it only costs like $2 $300 a month, which is pennies compared to how much you're forking out... on chatters
if you don't set it up correctly, you are simply going to make a shitty chatter

AI chat bots are making huge improvements very quickly and will soon become a dominant force in the market, delivering low latency, high quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest of Filipino virtual assistants
I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients

palm_comparison.vs_us: Directly maps to Palm's active AI-chatting project (§8 build-toward): defines a concrete tool pattern and the heavy-configuration requirement.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a configurable AI chat bot (Super Creator-class) for Palm's AI-chatting project, budgeting for heavy persona setup/training rather than expecting plug-and-play.

findings.title: Use an 'innocent yes-ladder' question to psychologically obligate the fan before sending a PPV

variants.claim: Asking 'Can I film a video for you, but I'm a bit shy — do you promise to go easy on me?' gets a yes-commitment that makes the fan feel obliged to purchase when the PPV arrives.
variants.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter asks a softly framed question that (a) gets a 'yes' and (b) maintains the model's innocent persona. The two components are explicit: 'can I film for you' = the commitment; 'I'm a bit shy' = innocence framing. Once the fan agrees, the PPV is sent. The felt obligation drives conversion.

variants.claim: Framing PPV offers as time-limited and personally exclusive ('only for you') reliably increases conversion rates.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct psychological levers are scripted: (1) urgency — 'only available for a few hours'; (2) exclusivity — 'I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special.' Both are delivered conversationally so they feel organic, not like a sales blast.

sources.quote: Can I film a video for you, but I'm a bit shy? Do you promise you will go easy on me?
he feels obliged that he has to buy it because he already said yes to filming the video
make the offer feel exclusive and time sensitive example I have something special for you but it's only available for a few hours
I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special I love talking to you

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). This is a specific pre-sale conversion mechanic not present in any Palm SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Incorporate a scripted yes-ladder template into the in-house chatter playbook as the mandatory bridge between rapport and first PPV send. A/B test the exact phrasing ('can I film for you' vs. 'I made something for you') and track conversion rate per chatter.

findings.title: PPV Looping: Follow up within 5–10 seconds of any purchase to chain sales

variants.claim: The best time to sell a subscriber another PPV is immediately after they make any purchase, capitalizing on the 'f*** it' impulse to keep the buying momentum going.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters monitor their CRM (e.g. Infloww) for any purchase notification — old PPV, mass PPV, feed post unlock — and follow up within 5–10 seconds with a curiosity-hook message before the fan even finishes watching what they bought. The follow-up doesn't reference the old video; it teases the next thing ('I was literally just about to do something I don't usually do'). From that reply, the chatter plans to sell 5 sequential PPVs, each progressively more explicit or longer, so each one appears more valuable and justifies a higher price. This loop restarts every time the fan buys.

variants.claim: After the first PPV purchase, cycling between descriptive text exchange, free teasers, and the next PPV sell maximizes session revenue; stopping the moment the fan finishes or runs out of money protects the long-term relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Post-first-purchase loop: (1) ask 'what would you do now if you saw me like this?' to get descriptive text, (2) mirror his fantasy back, (3) insert free teaser (photo/voice/short clip — not enough to satisfy), (4) set up next PPV with yes-ladder, (5) sell. Repeat until fan orgasms or explicitly says no money. At that point, immediately pivot to relationship-building mode — pre-recorded 'had a great time' appreciation video, romantic post-session questions.

sources.quote: the best time to sell something to a subscriber is right after they've made a purchase
chatters know the second they get that notification on their inflow or whatever CRM you use, that they need to follow up with the subscriber instantly
descriptive text, sound turned on, get descriptive text from him, make him imagine the scenario in his head, send free teasers, set up the next PPV, and sell the PPV
Sell until you can sell, right? Until he's tapped out or he comes.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced (§8). There is no internal SOP, chatter workflow, or CRM trigger process documented. This is a concrete, mechanics-level system that would form the core of Palm's future in-house chat team playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, make PPV looping the foundational selling framework. Define the CRM trigger (purchase notification), set a hard response-time SLA of <30 seconds, write curiosity-hook templates for the follow-up message, and require chatters to plan a 5-PPV ladder before entering any loop. Build this into chatter onboarding SOPs.

findings.title: Three-stage follow-up protocol for unopened/unpurchased PPVs — guilt-trip only as last resort

variants.claim: When a fan doesn't buy, a structured follow-up sequence (curiosity ping → reason discovery → resolution → guilt-trip last resort) recovers sales without burning the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Stage 1 — curiosity ping if left on seen: 'Where did you go?' / 'Don't just stare at it, you're making me nervous.' Stage 2 — if he replies but won't buy, ask why (trust issue, no money, bad timing) and resolve the specific objection (offer AI verification tip, suggest waiting). Stage 3 — only if he refuses to explain and is likely a time-waster: guilt-trip ('you made me film it and I'm not getting anything').

variants.claim: Sending a lone sad-face emoji to a subscriber who has gone quiet triggers guilt and reliably provokes a response, re-opening the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: When a subscriber has not replied to a previous message, instead of sending follow-up texts or more content pitches, the chatter sends only a '😢' or '🥺' emoji. Bjorn claims ~99.9% response rate. The mechanism: guilt-trips the subscriber into explaining their absence, which restarts rapport and spending. No spamming — one emoji only.

variants.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
variants.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.

sources.quote: If he left you on scene, you want to follow up, but don't be negative yet. Don't guilt trip. Use that as the last resort.
you want to find out why he isn't buying and resolve it

if a subscriber has not replied to your previous message and you want to get his attention simply just send a sad face emoji it is amazing how this will work I would say this probably works 99.9% of the time

do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). Palm has no documented follow-up or objection-handling flow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build this three-stage ladder into the in-house chatter SOP with explicit scripts for each stage. Flag time-wasters in the Fan Tracker (§9) after Stage 3 failure so chatters deprioritize them per the subscriber-priority queue (see finding on emoji tagging below).

findings.topic: Objection handling — add value before discounting
findings.title: When a subscriber objects to price, add more content first; only discount by removing content, not by lowering the price

variants.claim: Reflexively discounting when a subscriber says 'too expensive' trains them to always haggle; instead, offer more content at the same price first, and if a discount is unavoidable, remove content from the bundle rather than simply lowering the stated price.
variants.what_they_do: Rule 1 — Value-add first: if a sub resists price, add a small extra (bonus clip, picture set) and make it a huge deal, framing it as a personal favor. This gets full price paid and doesn't establish a discount expectation. Rule 2 — Renegotiation discount: if discounting is unavoidable, pre-load the PPV with a few extra pictures/clips by default. When the sub asks for less, remove those extras and drop the price — so the price reduction is tied to a concrete content reduction, not arbitrary. This preserves the perception that prices are meaningful. Rule 3 — Underpriced sub recovery: if a sub accepts a dropped price too readily, immediately propose a larger bundle ('I had an idea — I want to throw in extra stuff') to recapture upside.

variants.claim: When a fan objects to a custom content price, offering 50% upfront and 50% on delivery preserves the full price while reducing the perceived financial risk — avoiding the margin hit of a discount.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter expresses genuine excitement about the custom idea, adds her own suggestions to raise perceived effort/value, then quotes a premium price. On price objection: offer to deliver 'today/in 30 minutes' (urgency) and propose 50% now, 50% after delivery. Only drop the price as a final fallback if the 50/50 offer also fails.

sources.quote: it is a better first response to try and give them something small to sweeten the deal, and you get paid the full amount as opposed to just discounting
if they ask for a discount, you can take that stuff out and then drop the price, so you've now renegotiated
you can offer him 50/50. So he pays 50% before and 50% after
instead of dropping the price, you can tell him to pay half now, pay half later

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal objection-handling framework exists at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project, where objection-handling logic needs to be systematized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'add value before discounting, renegotiate via content removal' a non-negotiable chatter policy in the future in-house SOP. For the AI chatting project, encode this as a decision tree: objection detected → attempt value-add response → if second objection → remove content and lower price. Also document the underpriced-sub recovery move as a standard upsell trigger.

findings.topic: PPV price escalation ladders by fan tier
findings.title: Tiered PPV price escalation: 50% steps for low spenders, 70% steps for high spenders; break $200 cap via tip + multi-unlock

variants.claim: PPV prices should escalate by a set percentage each step within a loop, calibrated to the fan's spending tier, and the $200 OnlyFans cap is not a real ceiling — it can be bypassed via tips or multi-PPV unlocks.
variants.what_they_do: Low spenders (max purchase <$50): increase price ~50% per step. Example ladder: $35 → $55 → $85 → $125. High spenders (max purchase >$120): increase ~70% per step. Example ladder: $50 → $85 → $150 → $255. To exceed the $200 OF cap: (a) request a tip first, then send the PPV, or (b) split into multiple PPVs (e.g. 4×$200 = $800 total) framed as 'unlock these now and I'll send the main video while I get it from my archive.' Always frame multi-unlocks as a favor/bonus. If a subscriber accepts a dropped price too easily, immediately add more PPVs to the bundle to recover revenue ('I had an idea — because it's you, I want to throw in some extra stuff').

variants.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.

sources.quote: for low spenders, which I would define as someone with a maximum purchase of $50, we can increase the price each time by about 50%
for higher spenders, subscribers with a maximum purchase of over $120, we can try and increase the price by about 70% each time
you can't send a pay-per-view over $200. Sure, you can't, but you can still get around that in other ways
We'll start at 15 we'll then go to 25 we'll then go to 45 we'll then go to 65 and... 100 or 120

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or escalation framework (§7 GAP — explicitly called out as the #1 creator concern). This is directly applicable intelligence for building Palm's pricing playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document these two ladders (50% / 70% escalation by tier) as Palm's default PPV pricing framework. Add the $200-bypass mechanics (tip-first, multi-unlock bundle) to chatter SOPs. Critically, also adopt the 'add more content rather than discount' rule for objection handling — this protects price integrity over time and should be a standing chatter policy.

findings.title: Free-trial subs, monetize later via PPV — not on the sub price

variants.claim: Operators run free or near-free subscriptions to maximize entries, then make essentially all revenue from in-DM PPV and tips rather than the subscription fee.
variants.what_they_do: Run a 7-day free trial (or $3.99 floor sub) with scarcity framing ('limited spots / offers left'); the sub itself is treated as a bonus while the real money comes from getting fans inside and selling price-locked PPV scripts, tips, wall content and a recurring 'girlfriend experience' (e.g. $500/week VIP).

variants.claim: Legitimate agencies take no money upfront and no hidden fees, charging only via the revenue split, which is itself a trust differentiator at signing.
variants.what_they_do: Takes nothing upfront and no hidden fees, contrasting against agencies that make creators 'buy their way in'; frames the relationship as partners not assets and lays all terms out from the beginning so they never change.

sources.quote: I strongly, strongly stress that you run a free trial. You don't charge for subs
the girlfriend experience
nothing up front... I've been approached by a lot of other agencies and they all wanted me to buy my way into the agency
we really strive on being partners, you know, and not just treating you like an asset

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/sub model (§7 THIN); chatting/PPV that this depends on is outsourced (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the low-friction-entry + monetize-in-DM model and a VIP/girlfriend-experience recurring tier as inputs to Palm's pricing model (§7) and in-house/AI chat roadmap (§8).

findings.title: Hard floor of 50/50 revenue split — never go lower

variants.claim: Agencies should refuse any deal below a 50% commission split, framing it as the clean, fair baseline rather than a negotiable starting point.
variants.what_they_do: States a non-negotiable 50/50 split as the minimum: if a creator earns $2K/month, the agency takes $1K. No tiered structures, performance bonuses, or sliding scales are mentioned — flat 50% is presented as the standard.

variants.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
variants.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.

variants.claim: Charge around 50% for full-service management rather than discounting to a low percentage to win clients.
variants.what_they_do: Treats 50/50 as industry standard for doing everything (marketing + chatting); explicitly warns beginners against charging 20-30% out of desperation. Nuance: when starting with sub-$1k creators, stay around 40-60% (default 50%); higher 30-40% can fit only specific larger cases. Logic: getting a creator 1k->10k is harder than 37k->120k, so don't give away margin on the hard early work.

sources.quote: don't go below 50/50 revenue split. If your model makes $2,000 a month, that's 1,000 for her and 1,000 for you. Clean, fair, and simple.
you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline
charging like 20, 30, 35% when you're just starting out... the industry standard, go with 50% and you can't go wrong with that.
a 50/50 deal is quite common in the only fans management industry.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale, floor, or tier model (§7 GAP). The commission field exists in the Creators table but the logic behind what percentage to set is unwritten.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's commission policy explicitly: establish a floor (50% is industry-standard per this and other sources), define whether Palm ever takes less (e.g., for a high-revenue creator where 50% would be disproportionate) and codify the rationale. Add this to the creator onboarding wizard as a displayed policy, not just a blank field.

findings.title: Start subscribers on $15–$20 PPVs before escalating to $80–$100+

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
variants.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.

variants.claim: Selling PPV content below ~$20 for photos and ~$40 for videos trains fans to see the creator as cheap and destroys long-term monetization.
variants.what_they_do: This agency enforces hard price floors: no image PPV under $19.99, no video PPV under $39.99, no custom under $300. Low pricing is treated as a disqualifier, not a conversion tool. The logic is anchoring — cheap prices signal low value and attract fans who won't spend more, wasting chatter time for minimal return (framed as bad 'cash-for-effort' ratio).

sources.quote: you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100

if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars
you're now going to be selling pictures for no less than $19.99 each
Customs I'm not joking no less than $300

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or sequencing rules (§7 GAP, §8 outsourced). The external chat team may do this implicitly, but it is not a formalized Palm playbook. This is core build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a 3-tier PPV ladder in the chatter SOP: Tier 1 (entry, $15–$20) → Tier 2 (mid, $30–$50) → Tier 3 (high-ticket, $80–$100+). Require at least 2 Tier 1 purchases before a Tier 3 offer. Encode this sequencing into AI chatting logic when that project advances.

findings.title: Automate contract → onboarding form → calendar booking via Zapier

variants.claim: The moment a client fills out a sales intake form, a fully automated chain fires: contract sent via e-signatures.io, welcome email triggered on signing, onboarding form delivered, and kickoff calendar link sent — all without manual intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Three sequential Zapier workflows: (1) Notion sales intake form submission → e-signatures.io contract creation with placeholder fields auto-populated; (2) contract signed → Gmail welcome email with onboarding form link; (3) onboarding form submitted → Gmail email with GoHighLevel calendar booking link. Appointment reminders sent at 24hr, 1hr, and 5min via GHL automation. All built in Notion with a Zapier Pro account ($29.99/mo) and e-signatures.io pay-per-use ($0.49/contract).

variants.claim: A repeatable onboarding sequence — contract → account access → OF account audit → marketing setup → expectations meeting — prevents early relationship failures.
variants.what_they_do: After contract signing (via PandaDoc or DocuSign), agency logs into creator's OF account (coordinating 2FA in real time), audits and organizes the subscriber list by spend tier, organizes the content vault, sets up missing social platforms (IG, TikTok, Twitter), sends a content schedule, and holds an expectations-setting conversation covering response windows, platform norms, and month-1/2/3 milestones.

sources.quote: e signatures.io, it's not a $30 a month type of subscription to send my contracts. It is you pay per contract sent
sales intake form to contract. So, that's what we want to do
contract signed. Send onboarding form. Perfect. I'm going to go back to my folder
first step is to of course do the onboarding right so the onboarding entails a couple of different things

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a form wizard onboarding flow for creators (§6) and auto-generated PDF contracts via Puppeteer, but no documented automated chain that sequences contract → onboarding form → kickoff booking without manual steps. The creator-side onboarding is thin; there is no client/brand-side onboarding OS equivalent documented in Palm's baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build the same three-Zap chain for Palm's creator onboarding: sales intake form submission (Airtable or a form) triggers contract PDF (already Puppeteer-built), signing triggers an onboarding packet with a kickoff Calendly/GHL link, and form completion triggers a Telegram or email alert to Josh. Low incremental cost given existing Puppeteer contract system.

findings.topic: Loom-to-SOP pipeline for building a scalable SOP library
findings.title: Record a Loom, paste the auto-transcript into ChatGPT, and get a step-by-step SOP in minutes

variants.claim: The fastest way to build an SOP library is to record a Loom of yourself doing the task, copy the auto-generated transcript into ChatGPT with a 'turn this into a step-by-step SOP' prompt, and paste the result directly into a categorized Notion SOP database.
variants.what_they_do: Loom.com recording → copy transcript → ChatGPT prompt → formatted SOP pasted into Notion SOP library, tagged by department (sales, client success, hiring, organic marketing, etc.) and status (draft/active). SOPs are linked from daily winning formula tasks and role onboarding courses via hyperlinks. Cultural rule: when a team member asks a question, don't answer it directly — create an SOP around it, send the SOP, and store it. The SOP library is filtered into role-specific views so each team member sees only their relevant SOPs.

variants.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
variants.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.

sources.quote: what we like to do is we like to use a tool called Loom, loom.com, and we just film a video on what we're doing. You go to the video, go to transcript, copy the transcript, turn this into a SOP
anytime somebody asks a question, what we're not doing is we're not just going to answer the question right from the jump. We're just going to create an SOP around it
these are all the SOPs. This is sales, client success, hiring, etc.
Loom recordings

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented SOP library (§10 gap). The content pipeline (§1) is highly systematized in code and CLAUDE.md but not in a human-readable SOP format accessible to editors or Amin. The Loom-to-SOP method is extremely low-friction and matches Palm's current AI-assisted workflow culture.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Start Palm's SOP library with three highest-leverage SOPs: (1) editor submission-to-approval workflow, (2) Amin Telegram posting protocol, (3) creator content filming brief format. Use Loom + ChatGPT as described. Store in Airtable or a Notion page linked from the admin portal. Target: 10 SOPs in 2 weeks, then assign new SOPs as a recurring task whenever a process question is asked more than once.

findings.topic: KPI tracking and A/B testing culture
findings.title: Track all agency KPIs and A/B test every variable before committing to it

variants.claim: Knowing your exact PPV purchase rate, chatting ratio, average reply time, Instagram conversion, views-per-sub, and LTV — and A/B testing every script or tactic in 3+ variants — is the operational discipline that separates scaling agencies from stuck ones.
variants.what_they_do: Agency maintains a live spreadsheet where the owner can quote every core metric from memory or within 2 minutes. For any new script or tactic, they run 3 simultaneous variants across chatters, track unlock/purchase rates per variant, identify the winning element (e.g., a different opening angle), and roll that element into all other scripts. Testing is weekly and continuous.

variants.claim: A simple four-part operating system — weekly scorecard, creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and founder replacement ladder — is sufficient to escape the $100k/month trap without building a bloated corporate structure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's weekly scorecard covers four domains: (1) Revenue: creator gross, agency net, gross profit, owner profit, revenue per creator, top/declining performers, content compliance; (2) Acquisition: outreach sent, replies, qualified leads, booked calls, shows, closes, new creators signed; (3) Delivery: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator complaints; (4) Team: who hit targets, who missed, which bottleneck repeated, what was fixed. The delivery operating system specifically needs 7 pieces: weekly creator review, content output tracker, chat performance tracker, revenue breakdown, issues/bottleneck list, next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action. The founder replacement ladder moves any recurring founder task through: do it → document it → delegate it → review the output → metrics manage it → someone else fully owns it.

sources.quote: what's your PPV purchase rate, what's your chatting ratio, your average reply time, your Instagram conversion, how many views do you need to make per sub
you literally just need a very simple minimum viable operating system with four parts. A weekly scorecard, a creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and the founder replacement ladder

A simple delivery operating system needs seven pieces. A weekly creator review, a content output tracker, a chat performance tracker, a revenue breakdown, an issues and bottleneck list, and next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action.
you can't scale what you only feel. You scale what you can see every week.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has partial analytics infrastructure: the Whale Hunting dashboard and Fan Tracker CRM (§9 — PARTIAL) capture fan-side data, and the content pipeline tracks viral scores (§1 — MATURE). However, there is no documented agency-level KPI dashboard covering PPV purchase rate, chatter reply time, or IG conversion (§7 — GAP, §9 — GAP). A/B testing of chatting scripts is not possible today because chatting is outsourced (§8 — OUTSOURCED).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a single 'Agency Health' Airtable view or admin dashboard page tracking at minimum: IG follower growth rate, IG-to-OF conversion %, OF subscriber count, PPV purchase rate, and revenue per creator. As the in-house chat team is built (§8 roadmap), wire A/B test tracking into chatter SOP from day one — log script variant, message count, and revenue outcome per conversation.

findings.topic: Role onboarding with daily winning formula checklist
findings.title: Give every new hire a 'daily winning formula' — a repeating Notion task with linked SOPs for each duty

variants.claim: A repeating daily task assigned to each team role, listing every responsibility with embedded Loom SOPs or email templates, eliminates guesswork, prevents the employee from inventing their own process, and lets the founder delegate without micromanaging.
variants.what_they_do: For each role (editor, CSM, appointment setter, etc.): create a Notion task template called '[Role] Daily Winning Formula' that repeats every weekday. Each checklist item links to an SOP (Loom video transcript pasted into Notion) or has a toggle with an exact email template. Example CSM items: 'Maintain daily updates in Notion by 10am', 'Review and QC all video content within 4 hours of submission', 'Send content to clients for approval', 'Update client portal'. Task auto-populates each morning. Pair with a 30-day onboarding course (gallery of Loom recordings) and pre-day-1 through day-6 onboarding checklist.

variants.claim: Without an SOP for every role, a new hire requires weeks of founder handholding, negating the time savings of hiring and blocking agency growth.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis and his partner documented every job into SOPs before delegating. They reinvested 100% of revenue for the first 12 months into the next hire, next system, or software — taking no personal salary. The founder's job is specifically to build the SOP so the hire can self-onboard and replace the founder in that function.

sources.quote: daily winning formula is basically what we'll do with this... I make it a repeating task every weekday... this is a task that just populates every single day for the client success manager
the reason your team would suck is because you're not actually equipping them with things like this to actually do well
every time I found myself doing something repeatedly, I basically just documented it, systematized it using Notion, and then trained someone else to handle it
if you do not have an SOP for every single part of your business, every single job, every single role, then again, you're never going to make it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Clerk roles (editor, ai_editor, chat_manager, creator) and an editor workflow with a day-counter task queue (§1, §10), but no documented daily winning formula, role-specific onboarding checklist, or SOP library linked to recurring tasks. The §10 gap notes no hiring criteria or scaling plan. This tactic is immediately applicable to Palm's editors and Amin (contractor posting role).
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's two most active roles — editor and Amin (manual posting) — create a daily Airtable or Notion checklist with 5–8 items each, linked to existing SOPs (or quick Loom recordings). Specifically for the editor: items like 'check task queue, pull next 2 slots, upload to Cloudflare, submit for approval.' For Amin: 'check Telegram topic, post per schedule, reply /posted.' This is a low-effort, high-leverage move to reduce dependency on Josh/Evan for day-to-day direction.

findings.title: DM prospects from a personal-looking account, not an agency page

variants.claim: Run creator outreach from a personal-looking profile rather than a branded agency account so messages get read.
variants.what_they_do: Send from your personal/duplicated account (lifestyle pics, normal-person vibe), never an 'OFM manager' agency account, because models skim their request folder and skip anything that reads as an agency. Stop labeling yourself 'OFM manager' anywhere; call it 'talent/influencer management' instead so you aren't lumped in with the spam.

variants.claim: Setting up a credible Instagram profile with professional photos (not casual/topless) is the primary vehicle for cold-outreaching creators, and showing your face builds trust that converts to signed deals.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner creates a personal IG account with polished location/lifestyle photos (real or Pinterest-sourced). Shows face for credibility; faceless is acceptable if IG photos are professional. DMs creators who appear stuck, plateaued, or post-bad-agency. Offers a free 1-week trial to prove value, then converts to 50/50. Moves promising leads to FaceTime to close.

sources.quote: most people send their messages out from like an agency account... I would actually highly advise you to do your personal account.
please stop saying OFM manager in either your username, your bio... try to be a little bit different... talent management agency.
you want to just make an Instagram profile, you want to set it up some nice business photos
you can offer if you find a model and you believe in yourself… I'll work for free for you for a week and if things improve then we can go to a 50/50 split

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led + referrals with no documented outreach-profile standard (§6 THIN/GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize an outreach profile spec (personal-style account, lifestyle content, non-'OFM' positioning) as part of Palm's acquisition playbook.

findings.topic: Cold outreach via Instagram personal brand
findings.title: Build a professional IG persona to run cold outreach to creators

variants.claim: Setting up a credible Instagram profile with professional photos (not casual/topless) is the primary vehicle for cold-outreaching creators, and showing your face builds trust that converts to signed deals.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner creates a personal IG account with polished location/lifestyle photos (real or Pinterest-sourced). Shows face for credibility; faceless is acceptable if IG photos are professional. DMs creators who appear stuck, plateaued, or post-bad-agency. Offers a free 1-week trial to prove value, then converts to 50/50. Moves promising leads to FaceTime to close.

variants.claim: Run creator outreach from a personal-looking profile rather than a branded agency account so messages get read.
variants.what_they_do: Send from your personal/duplicated account (lifestyle pics, normal-person vibe), never an 'OFM manager' agency account, because models skim their request folder and skip anything that reads as an agency. Stop labeling yourself 'OFM manager' anywhere; call it 'talent/influencer management' instead so you aren't lumped in with the spam.

sources.quote: you want to just make an Instagram profile, you want to set it up some nice business photos
you can offer if you find a model and you believe in yourself… I'll work for free for you for a week and if things improve then we can go to a 50/50 split
most people send their messages out from like an agency account... I would actually highly advise you to do your personal account.
please stop saying OFM manager in either your username, your bio... try to be a little bit different... talent management agency.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound (§6) but no documented IG persona setup, photo standard, or structured funnel from IG profile → DM → FaceTime → sign. The free-trial-then-sign tactic is not in Palm's playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a Palm acquisition IG profile standard: photo style, bio copy, niche positioning. Add a free-trial offer as a low-friction entry for creators already earning but skeptical. Build a short outreach sequence: IG DM → value hook → FaceTime close → contract. Cheap to implement, directly addresses §6 gap.

findings.title: Hold weekly video calls with each creator to give account updates and collect feedback

variants.claim: A recurring weekly Zoom/video call with the model — showing results, flagging problems honestly, and requesting content adjustments — is the primary relationship-maintenance mechanism.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a standing weekly call per model on a video platform (Zoom or equivalent). The call covers: current account performance update (good and bad news), requested content changes for the coming week, and any operational issues. The visual face-to-face element is cited as relationship-strengthening beyond what async messaging achieves.

variants.claim: Sending a net promoter score survey to all clients on the first of every month, routing responses into a tracked database, and requiring the CSM to respond to every score — including passives and detractors — directly improves retention and provides data to improve the product.
variants.what_they_do: Notion form (NPS survey) with name, email, score (0–10), and open comment. Repeating task on the 1st of each month assigned to CSM with the form link and a pre-written email template embedded in the task. On submission: Notion automation sends internal team email alert and a thank-you email to the client. Scores roll up to the client OS (average NPS per client visible at a glance). CSM board shows unresponded scores filtered by 'responded = unchecked.' Company-wide average NPS tracked on client success dashboard. Threshold: any score below 8 triggers immediate action review.

sources.quote: set up a Cadence of calls like on a weekly basis where you get on a zoom or get on some sort of call with the model you see them face to face and you explain what's going on
NPS. It's called net promoter score. Detractors, passives, promoters. We want to set up a system where we can send this out once per month
we don't want somebody to fill out an NPS form and then we don't respond
if this is below 8, below 9, I need to focus on this. I need to like dial this in like really really hardcore

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator success cadence or QBR rhythm (§9 flags this as a gap). Communication is through Telegram per §5. There is no standing weekly call structure with creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a standing weekly 20-min creator check-in call. Build a lightweight call template in Notion: (1) last-week metrics summary pulled from OF dashboard, (2) content requests for next week, (3) any platform/account issues, (4) open Q&A. Log call notes back to the Creators Airtable record. This directly fills the §9 retention/churn gap.

findings.topic: Creator relationship management and milestone celebration
findings.title: Assign a dedicated model-facing manager to celebrate creator milestones and prevent churn

variants.claim: Proactively recognizing creator milestones (first viral video, top 1%, highest-earning month) via a dedicated relationship manager — not the CEO — is a primary driver of creator retention and prevents the agency-creator 'butting heads' dynamic.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe a specific failure mode: the agency founder misses milestone moments (e.g., creator hits first six figures) because they're buried in ops. The fix is a model-facing manager whose explicit job includes celebrating wins, providing comfortability, and making the creator feel involved in the agency. Examples given: 'you just hit the top 1%,' 'your highest month ever,' 'first 1M video.' This person also handles weekly or daily content feedback, direction relays, and expectation-setting — keeping creators informed and emotionally invested in the partnership.

variants.claim: Sending a net promoter score survey to all clients on the first of every month, routing responses into a tracked database, and requiring the CSM to respond to every score — including passives and detractors — directly improves retention and provides data to improve the product.
variants.what_they_do: Notion form (NPS survey) with name, email, score (0–10), and open comment. Repeating task on the 1st of each month assigned to CSM with the form link and a pre-written email template embedded in the task. On submission: Notion automation sends internal team email alert and a thank-you email to the client. Scores roll up to the client OS (average NPS per client visible at a glance). CSM board shows unresponded scores filtered by 'responded = unchecked.' Company-wide average NPS tracked on client success dashboard. Threshold: any score below 8 triggers immediate action review.

sources.quote: highlighting those Milestones is big for sure like hey let's say you have a new Creator right you tell her hey we just hit the top 1% like congrats or like let's say her she just hit her highest month ever
NPS. It's called net promoter score. Detractors, passives, promoters. We want to set up a system where we can send this out once per month
we don't want somebody to fill out an NPS form and then we don't respond
if this is below 8, below 9, I need to focus on this. I need to like dial this in like really really hardcore

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator retention strategy or milestone-tracking cadence (§9 gap). Whale-hunting and Fan Tracker CRM exist for fan-side retention but not creator-side. Creator communication runs through Josh directly today (§10), which doesn't scale and leaves milestone moments to chance. No win-back or churn-signal framework exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As the model-manager role is built out (see delegation finding above), explicitly include milestone-celebration and retention cadence in the job spec: weekly check-in call or message, milestone alerts triggered from revenue dashboard (e.g., Airtable rule when monthly earnings hit new high), and a standard 'congrats' communication template. Add a creator-churn-risk signal to the Airtable Creators table (e.g., days since last communication, revenue trend direction) to surface at-risk creators proactively.

findings.topic: Fan connection before subscription as LTV driver
findings.title: Build parasocial fan connection via social persona BEFORE the OF subscribe to raise LTV

variants.claim: Fans who feel connected to a creator's persona — trusting her, finding her funny, being attracted to her personality — before they subscribe spend more and stay longer, measurably raising LTV 20–30% vs. content-first pages.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin's agency builds a distinct social persona for each creator so fans are 'invested before they even spend money.' The persona is expressed across IG/TikTok (top-of-funnel), and the brand promise is consistent with what fans find on OF. This is tracked: they observe LTV and CVR lift of 20–30% after implementing persona-first strategy.

variants.claim: Sending a warm, smiling 'thank you' video after a fan's spending session — when he is emotionally most vulnerable — builds the relationship bond that drives repeat monetization rather than churn.
variants.what_they_do: When a fan signals spending fatigue, the chatter stops pushing PPVs entirely. Instead, the model sends a pre-filmed 'gratification video': short clip of her smiling, saying she had a great time, expressing genuine-seeming warmth. The chatter then attempts to extend the chat slightly rather than letting the fan leave immediately. The logic: the fan is emotionally open at this moment; a positive close imprints a good memory that makes him return for the full PPV ladder again next session.

sources.quote: if you can create that connection before the only fans like they're invested already before they even spend money that's what creates retention
I've seen my LTV go up like 20 30% along with CVR along with all these other metrics

this is just a simple video of the model saying thank you and that she had a really good time to the camera being happy smiling. So you make the fan feel good. This is where they're most vulnerable to relationship building
you repeat this process until the fan can't spend anymore. When the fan can't spend anymore, you don't want to force any more sales because this will just ruin the whole relationship

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline (§1) is strong on viral content distribution but the baseline doc does not document a persona-consistency check between social identity and OF page experience. Fan retention strategy (§9) tracks whales and going-cold signals but no written retention playbook exists. The persona-to-LTV linkage is not captured.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a 'brand continuity' checkpoint: after the Creator Identity step (see content finding above), ensure the OF bio, welcome message, and pinned PPV messaging reflect the same persona defined in social.
