Document this in the onboarding SOP (§6). For §9 fan retention, add persona-fit as a variable when analyzing whale and churn data.

findings.title: Give high spenders 1:1 personalized treatment, never mass messages

variants.claim: Retain top spenders by excluding them from mass blasts and giving escalating personalized touches as they cross spend thresholds.
variants.what_they_do: Tier fans by spend on a smart list; anyone over a threshold (e.g. $1,500) gets zero mass messages/PPVs, only personalized 1:1. Touches escalate: >$500 first night = personalized voice note (real or ElevenLabs AI); always call them by their saved nickname; >$2,500/mo = a short safe-for-work selfie video saying their name; occasional physical-prize raffles. One client made $56,500 in 30 days from her top 5 fans.

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: for big spending fans. You don't send them mass messages or mass pay-per-views... you want to give them that one-on-one personal interaction.
Anybody over $500 in their first night should get personalized voice notes... we can use AI voice notes... 11 Labs.
If the fan has spent more than $2.5,000 in one month, record a short personalized safer work selfie video saying his name.
prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale tooling (Fan Tracker, going-cold detection, smart-list-style data) but no written whale-nurture playbook (§9 PARTIAL/GAP); chatting is outsourced (§8), so the tactics are build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Turn whale data into an operating playbook: spend-threshold tiers, exclude-from-mass rule, nickname use, voice-note/selfie-video escalations (AI voice where applicable), raffles — build-toward for in-house/AI chat.

findings.title: Productized fulfillment makes the creator the only variable

variants.claim: Standardizing the entire delivery system so the creator is the only variable is what makes an agency scalable, reducing dependence on any single creator.
variants.what_they_do: Runs every avatar-fit creator through one standardized machine (onboarding, evergreen content verticals, welcome message, funnel, chat monetization) auto-triggered on signing; same delivery took one creator 12k to 282k/mo and another to 44k/mo with no extra custom work, at 1.6M/mo and 60% margins. Onboarding is 'complete' only after the first full week of delivery.

variants.claim: Agencies that maintain traffic through IG downturns retain their creators; agencies that can't keep traffic flowing lose models and revenue simultaneously.
variants.what_they_do: This agency explicitly frames multi-channel traffic infrastructure (Meta ads + phone farm) not just as a growth tool but as a creator relationship tool. When IG accounts get restricted, creators see revenue drop and blame the agency. By having non-IG traffic running continuously, the agency absorbs IG volatility without it becoming a creator-facing crisis. The result: agency kept all models during a period when competitors lost creators, and received referrals from satisfied models.

sources.quote: Productized fulfillment just means the same delivery model for every creator and we're allowing the creator to be the variable
one will go on to make 280k, one will go on to make 44k... and the creator is the variable
Models are leaving, your revenue is going down... we've been able to keep all of our models, even get referrals, whilst most agencies are losing their creators
you should be providing them traffic even when things are bad

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline is productized (§1 MATURE) but OF vault/PPV and chat delivery are not standardized (§2 THIN, §8 OUTSOURCED); no single end-to-end fulfillment machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's productized social pipeline into a full standardized fulfillment system (page setup, welcome message, funnel, chat) so new creators plug into one machine and revenue isn't concentrated in a few creators.

findings.title: Build a perpetual stockpile of karma-farmed Reddit accounts via VA team

variants.claim: Running Reddit at scale requires a dedicated VA farming team that continuously creates and seasons accounts to a minimum karma threshold so you always have ready inventory to rotate in when accounts get banned.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a team of VAs (plus automation) that create Reddit accounts daily, warm them for 2-3 days acting as normal users, then farm post karma (target: 2,000) and comment karma (target: 300) by posting in meme/advice subreddits. Accounts are stockpiled; when a posting account gets banned a seasoned replacement is pulled immediately from the pile. VAs are paid per account that hits the karma threshold. Automation now supplements VAs for scale.

variants.claim: Creating Reddit accounts from scratch and warming them organically produces longer-lasting accounts than buying, because device-fingerprint mismatches on purchased accounts trigger faster bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency creates accounts fresh, runs a 3–5 day warmup (scroll, join subreddits, 1–2 upvotes/downvotes/comments per day), then begins posting. Bought accounts are acknowledged as faster but riskier. Each account gets a unique tracking/trial link, unique content (spoofed), and unique captions so no two accounts can be chain-banned via shared signals.

sources.quote: we have an account creation system every day. there's new accounts being created and there's just a farming team
An account is done seasoning if it hits 2,000 post karma and 300 common karma
we run the accounts on mobile… mobile Reddit is a lot safer and the accounts last a lot longer than they would on web
the link should be different every account… nothing should link a different account to another because that's how you can get chain banned

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit strategy at all (§5/§2 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). This is pure build-from-zero territory. Palm's Apify scraping and Publer infra exists for IG/TikTok but nothing maps to Reddit account farming or karma seasoning.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm adds Reddit to its traffic stack, do NOT start with black-hat scale. Begin with the expert's own minimum viable advice: one dedicated VA, one phone, one account per creator, organic posting to well-matched subreddits daily. Only invest in a farming pipeline after proving per-creator ROI. Document karma thresholds (2,000 post / 300 comment) as acceptance criteria for any farmed account.

findings.title: Bootstrap Threads from existing IG account for follower carry-over

variants.claim: Creating a Threads account from an existing Instagram account gives an immediate follower boost that accelerates early growth.
variants.what_they_do: TDM connects each creator's existing IG account to Threads so that existing Instagram followers automatically carry over at account creation, providing an instant audience base rather than starting from zero.

variants.claim: High early posting frequency on Threads accelerates follower growth because the platform still incentivises new content volume.
variants.what_they_do: TDM posts 5–8 times per day per Threads account in the first weeks. Once the account hits 1,000 followers (or after a couple of weeks), they reduce cadence to 3–5 posts per day. They mix carousel posts (highest performers) with shorter comment/text posts as filler.

variants.claim: Threads algorithmically amplifies brand-new accounts' first posts, making it faster to gain initial traction than Twitter/X, where momentum takes much longer to build.
variants.what_they_do: Create a new Threads account, post immediately — the first post gets organic reach from Threads' push. Content is simple engaging text (e.g. 'somebody FaceTime me' style posts targeting lonely men). Bio links to Telegram rather than directly to Fanvue, then Telegram DMs are used to warm and convert. Positioned as a 'hidden gem' due to platform newness.

sources.quote: use an account that you already have on Instagram to make the threads account because this will give you a boost, a kickstart from all the followers that you have coming over
5 to eight posts per day per account. Now, after a couple of weeks or when you reach 1,000 followers, you can back off a little bit to three, maybe five posts a day
Threads is really underrated. It can bring in a lot of traffic especially right now because it's more of a newer platform
when you make a brand new Threads account or get a Threads account right off the bat, your first post is going to get views

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Threads strategy (§5 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). Palm does run IG accounts for creators (§1) but there is no recorded practice of extending those to Threads.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm sets up IG accounts for real creators, simultaneously create a linked Threads account to capture the follower carry-over benefit at no extra cost. Add Threads to the Phase 3 distribution rollout (batch-5).

findings.title: Three-platform funnel stack: IG → TikTok → Threads, each with distinct path

variants.claim: Instagram, TikTok, and Threads each require a different funnel path to convert views into paying fans, and together they cover the highest-ROI free traffic sources for AI models.
variants.what_they_do: IG funnel: views → tagged slave account in bio → link in bio → Fanvue → chat → sale. TikTok funnel: views → IG handle in bio (no adult links allowed) → IG bio link → Fanvue. Threads funnel: views → Telegram link in bio → Telegram DM → Fanvue. Each platform's content format and policy dictates the redirect chain.

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: we're only going to go over Instagram, Tik Tok, and threads because all the money I've made so far has been through these three
Funnel one, Instagram. the path they follow, views, tagged account in bio, link in bio, fan view, chatting, and sales
are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is not documented (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm uses Publer + Telegram routing for posting but no written multi-platform funnel architecture exists. Palm's real-creator social pipeline (§1) covers IG/TikTok content production but not the cross-platform redirect logic.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document explicit per-platform funnel paths for both real and AI creators. For real creators, IG bio → Linktree/Beacons → OF is likely already in use informally; codify it. For the AI persona line (§3), adopt the TikTok→IG→OF redirect chain since TikTok blocks adult links directly.

findings.title: 7-day IG warm-up: algorithm training, niche engagement, delayed link

variants.claim: New social accounts are warmed for about a week of human-like activity and niche engagement before any adult link is added, to build trust score and avoid early bans.
variants.what_they_do: Day-by-day: matching Gmail/name, scroll+like niche reels to train the algorithm, neutral (non-model) profile pic, follow ~15 niche creators/day, engage and like older US/UK 'simp' commenters under competitor posts to pull them over (never DM), then post first reel ~day 7 and only add the link.bio funnel 2-4 weeks in when followers justify the risk.

variants.claim: Bought aged IG accounts need only a 3–5 day warmup (engaging OF creator posts, scrolling) before going live — longer warmups recommended by others are unnecessary in practice.
variants.what_they_do: Purchase aged IG accounts to skip karma/history building. Warmup = 3–5 days of scrolling and engaging with OF creator content only. Then set up bio with link.me or Linktree, profile photo (face + slight chest), pin three posts showing key body parts relevant to niche. Post 2 Reels/day. For account isolation: UK/US/AU/CA creators can use home Wi-Fi unless ban rate exceeds ~10 accounts; otherwise use VPN/proxy.

sources.quote: how you set up and warm up an Instagram properly within 7 days to avoid being banned
Build up your Instagram account slowly and patiently
we buy our accounts aged and then we do a warm up for around like… 3 to 5 day warm up of just interacting OF creators engaging with the posts scrolling and that's it
IG is not as strict as people think it is… if you're in the UK, US, Australia, Canada… then you can use mobile Wi-Fi

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature and fully specified (§4 90-day cadence) but real-creator warmup is a documented gap (§4 THIN); this gives a concrete short-form warmup playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adapt a documented short warm-up + delayed-link checklist for new real-creator IG accounts (algorithm training, niche engagement, no-DM rule, risk-to-reward link timing) to fill the §4 real-creator gap.

findings.topic: Revenue vs profit distinction at scale
findings.title: High revenue means nothing without margin — $100k revenue can yield $3k profit

variants.claim: Agencies can hit $100,000/month in revenue while netting as little as $3,000 in profit, so raw revenue targets are misleading without tracking margin.
variants.what_they_do: TDM explicitly warns against 'brute forcing' to $50–70k/month and treating it as success; they frame margin as the true health metric, arguing that low-margin agencies cannot reinvest in better team, systems, or experimentation, which caps growth.

variants.claim: Creator gross revenue is the least useful number for judging agency health; founders must track five sequential layers down to owner profit per hour.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan defines a 5-level waterfall: (1) creator gross, (2) agency net after split, (3) gross profit after fulfillment costs, (4) owner profit after everything, (5) owner profit per hour. He insists the only number that matters is what the founder actually keeps, and that 'revenue is vanity, profit is sanity, cash is reality.' He frames agencies claiming $100k/month as often netting $5-30k for the founder after chatters, managers, editors, VAs, software, promo, and mistakes are paid.

sources.quote: some agencies will do $100,000 in revenue and $3,000 in profit
level two is the agency net revenue. So that is what you keep after the creator split
level four is owner profit. So this is what you take home after everything. This is the number that actually counts
level five is ultimately the one I would call the good metric. The owner profit per hour

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented internal baseline for margin targets or profit-vs-revenue tracking (§7 GAP). Weekly invoices are auto-computed but there is no documented P&L framework or margin floor that triggers a scaling decision.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a minimum margin % threshold (e.g., 30–40% net) as an internal KPI alongside gross revenue. Build a simple P&L view into the admin portal or Airtable so Josh and Evan can see whether revenue growth is actually profitable before committing to new hires or infrastructure.

findings.topic: Sequential creator scaling vs. spreading across many low earners
findings.title: Max out one creator fully before onboarding the next; avoid managing many small creators simultaneously

variants.claim: Agencies stuck at $20K/mo with 8–9 small creators ($2–5K each) are less profitable and harder to scale than agencies with 3–4 creators each maximized sequentially; focus on one creator until capped, then move to the next.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin explicitly advises: sign one creator, put all focus on growing her to maximum revenue, then only take on the next creator when bandwidth permits. Barry runs 4 creators with one responsible for ~$50K alone. The agency avoids the trap of signing volume for its own sake. Paid promo layered on top of organic is used to stabilize revenue so the agency isn't 'one bad algorithm month away from losing everything.'

variants.claim: Reaching the first 10k/month comes from running the business as a real systemized operation—day-one onboarding, scripts, chatting, marketing all in place—and perfecting one creator before adding the next.
variants.what_they_do: Insists onboarding (Drive, content sheet/niche, scripts) be completed on day one not stretched over a week, gets Infloww scripts and smart messages set up before a chatter starts, focuses one marketing channel, and only scales to the next creator once one is fully systemized.

sources.quote: they have like nine creators all at like two three four five it's like just a bunch of creators
I get one model and I'll just try to max it out as much as possible and then from there when I feel ready and I have time enough to do it I'll just move on to the next one
It needs to all be done on day one
Perfect that for one model. Scale to the next

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's creator acquisition is Josh-DM-led (§6) with no documented sequential scaling model or creator revenue concentration strategy. There is no internal guideline on how many creators to manage at once or what revenue threshold triggers signing the next creator. This is a planning/founder-discipline gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's creator scaling model: set a minimum monthly revenue threshold per creator (e.g., $15K–$20K/mo stable for 60 days) before signing the next creator. Document this as part of the acquisition SOP (§6). Also evaluate current creator portfolio for revenue concentration — if one creator dominates, build a risk-mitigation plan (paid promo layer, brand depth) before pursuing new signings.

findings.topic: Platform-native content formats for AI model social
findings.title: Format-match by platform: long-caption storytelling on IG, slideshows on TikTok, text posts on Threads

variants.claim: Each platform rewards a specific content format; matching format to platform algorithm dramatically increases reach and watch time for AI model accounts.
variants.what_they_do: Instagram: long-caption 'storytelling' posts where the persona narrates a relatable story (ex-drama etc.) — increases watch time which triggers broader algorithmic push; captions generated via ChatGPT from real OFI model examples. Also 'caption hook videos' with an on-screen text hook. TikTok: slideshow format prioritized because TikTok is actively promoting slides and they go viral more easily; targets lonely-men content angles. Threads: short punchy text posts ('somebody FaceTime me') that feel real and prompt engagement from the target demographic.

variants.claim: Posting 2–3 Reels per day split between algorithm-maintenance lifestyle content and virality-engineered vertical content is the primary Instagram growth method for driving OF subscriptions.
variants.what_they_do: 2–3 Reels/day. Filler content = lifestyle/personal content to keep the account active and algorithm-fed. Vertical content = explicitly engineered for virality — Snapchat-style verticals, talking-head videos, mirror videos. Both types use 'link in bio' and pinned comments directing to OF. Competitor analysis is central: identify top-performing competitor videos, isolate the 3 best, test posting similar styles, then use performance data to teach the creator what to film and why it works (lighting, facial expression, movement).

sources.quote: Find some good storytelling post from OFI models on IG and use chat GTP to make your own
when it comes to Tik Tok you're going to want to do more slides because Tik Tok really favors slides
Filler content is just going to be her lifestyle. This keeps the algorithm happy… Vertical content is what brings new people into your page
Find three competitors. Make a list of the top performing videos. Take the three most performing videos. Put that in a style selection process

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong at inspo-scraping and replicating viral Reels but is built around video for IG. The Airtable pipeline does not appear to have a Threads text-post workflow or TikTok slideshow format lane. Content format strategy is not documented per-platform.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's content pipeline (§1) to include platform-specific format lanes: (1) TikTok slideshow template workflow, (2) Threads text-post templates. For real creators, these formats can supplement the existing Reels-first approach. For AI personas, add ChatGPT-assisted long-caption storytelling as a content type in the `Inspiration`/`Assets` pipeline.

findings.topic: Social content strategy — filler vs. vertical content split
findings.title: Two-content-type IG framework: 'filler' (lifestyle) keeps algorithm happy; 'vertical' (viral-engineered) drives new traffic

variants.claim: Posting 2–3 Reels per day split between algorithm-maintenance lifestyle content and virality-engineered vertical content is the primary Instagram growth method for driving OF subscriptions.
variants.what_they_do: 2–3 Reels/day. Filler content = lifestyle/personal content to keep the account active and algorithm-fed. Vertical content = explicitly engineered for virality — Snapchat-style verticals, talking-head videos, mirror videos. Both types use 'link in bio' and pinned comments directing to OF. Competitor analysis is central: identify top-performing competitor videos, isolate the 3 best, test posting similar styles, then use performance data to teach the creator what to film and why it works (lighting, facial expression, movement).

variants.claim: Reels are the primary growth lever and each is engineered with a 3-second hook, mid retention and a payoff, produced at 2-3/day split across a few fixed content pillars.
variants.what_they_do: Treats reels as the top of the IG pecking order (reels > posts > stories > funnel); structures each as hook (first 3s) / retention / reward (e.g. an ugly-to-hot transformation); defines ~3 niche content pillars and split-tests them at 2-3 reels/day, doubling down on winners and cutting losers; moved from static text-on-image reels to 'clean motion' video.

sources.quote: Filler content is just going to be her lifestyle. This keeps the algorithm happy… Vertical content is what brings new people into your page
Find three competitors. Make a list of the top performing videos. Take the three most performing videos. Put that in a style selection process
Every reel should have a hook, a retention and a reward
a minimum of two quality reels per day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature 8-step inspo-driven pipeline that uses viral data to decide what creators film (§1), which directly parallels the competitor analysis piece here. However, Palm's posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is not codified (§1 GAP). The filler vs. vertical content taxonomy is not explicitly documented in Palm's system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formally adopt the filler/vertical taxonomy inside Palm's content pipeline. Tag each Asset in Airtable as 'filler' or 'vertical/traffic-driver' so editors and schedulers can enforce the 2–3/day cadence with the right mix. This codifies the cadence GAP noted in §1.

findings.title: Real models beat AI on connection, customs and platform access

variants.claim: AI personas underperform real creators because most OFM revenue comes from personal connection and custom content, and AI is pushed off OnlyFans onto less reliable platforms.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI-model courses are 'selling shovels in a gold rush', that the real money is in fans believing they're talking to a real girl and in customs AI can't do, that OnlyFans won't host AI so you're forced to Fanvue (which pays out unreliably), and that referrals from happy real creators are the best growth engine.

variants.claim: Treat an AI creator's from-scratch follower build and 'too-perfect to be real' suspicion as core risks, and prefer scaling a real creator with existing momentum.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI OFM is overhyped: content is limited (close-to-camera, props/Ferrari emblems look wrong, constant proof-checking, costs per generation), no real customs, no collabs/podcasts/in-person viral content, and you start from zero followers where a '10/10' new account looks suspicious. His own AI creator scaled to only ~$25k with huge effort; he prefers taking 50% of a real creator's fast growth over 100% of little.

sources.quote: they are all pretty much trying to sell you shovels in a gold rush
if you don't get paid, you don't get paid
you're going to be starting with an AI creator from scratch. like zero followers... how suspect it looks if your AI creator is like a 10 out of 10 chick.
we scaled a creator to $25,000. But the amount of time, energy, and effort that took. It is just not worth it compared to just working with real life creators.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Tempers Palm's AI-persona thesis (§0/§3): Palm's AI personas still funnel real social connection to OF, but this flags the customs/whale and platform-access limits and the Fanvue payout risk for any future AI-creator line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep Palm's AI personas as a social/scaling lever while concentrating customs/whale monetization on real creators; treat Fanvue-class platforms and payout reliability as explicit risks for any AI-creator expansion.

findings.title: AI personas remove model-dependency risk and keep 100% of revenue

variants.claim: Operators argue an owned synthetic creator beats managing real talent because it removes model-dependency, agency-hopping and split-revenue risk while keeping all earnings.
variants.what_they_do: Frames traditional OFM as a 'casino' (lazy models, theft, unpaid invoices, agency hoppers) where a $24k month nets only ~$6.5k after split; an AI model on Fanvue keeps 100% (minus 20% platform fee) for ~$17.5k net, fully owner-controlled and self-reliant.

variants.claim: AI creators can't use OnlyFans, so run them on an AI-friendly platform like Fanvue and prioritize realistic presentation over obvious perfection.
variants.what_they_do: OnlyFans does not allow AI; Fanvue welcomes AI, lets you label the account AI, and takes 15% vs OF's 20%. Build the persona like Build-A-Bear (face to niche, then body), batch content for both social and the paid platform, make the IG bio/handle look like a real person (uni, city, age), then funnel reels traffic to Fanvue and monetize via subscription + tips + PPV. Same can scale to ~$3-8k/mo on one model with no revenue split.

sources.quote: you get 100% share obviously with this business model
It's a casino because that's basically what it is
Only Fans does not allow AI models. It takes 20% fees... Fan View... they advocate AI creators. They take 15%.
The more realistic you make it, the better your results will be... bio that maybe says which university they're in... which city that they live in.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs real creators on OnlyFans today with revenue split (§0) and treats AI personas as a future scaling lever (§3 MATURE). Palm's AI personas still funnel to OnlyFans via social, unlike the AI-creator/Fanvue model taught here.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Hold for the future AI-creator line: validate the owner-keeps-100% Fanvue economics as the business case for a Palm AI line, separate from the real-creator OnlyFans core.

findings.title: Never send mass PPVs — individual messaging generates 3-4x more revenue

variants.claim: Mass PPVs lose far more money than they make because the non-buyers (majority) could have been converted through individual, personalized outreach.
variants.what_they_do: They avoid mass PPV blasts entirely. Instead, chatters message each subscriber individually, open with small talk to warm them up, then present content as if it was created specifically for that fan. The argument: of 1,000 subscribers who receive a $20 mass PPV, ~50 buy; but of the 950 who didn't, 100–200 more could be closed through 1-on-1 conversation — revenue that is permanently lost with mass sends.

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

sources.quote: if you send mass ppvs yes you will make some money... the problem is you lose way more money than you actually do
it is never and trust me on that never a good idea to send mass ppvs... just chat with them yourself you will make 10 times the money minimum

you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal SOP on PPV delivery method. No documented position on mass vs. individual PPV exists. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, institute a hard policy against mass PPV blasts. Default all PPV delivery to individual chat sequences. Use this framing in chatter training: mass PPV = lazy; individual = 10x revenue floor.

findings.topic: OF vault folder structure for chatter efficiency
findings.title: Organise OF vault into named script folders so chatters can locate PPV assets instantly

variants.claim: Structuring the OF content vault into named folders (e.g. 'Script 1', 'New Subscriber', 'PPV', 'SFW Feed') tied to specific chat scripts reduces chatter errors and speeds up PPV delivery during live conversations.
variants.what_they_do: Inside the OF vault the agency creates dedicated folders for: (1) each reusable sexting script (Script 1, Script 2, etc.) containing all media needed for that session; (2) a 'New Subscriber' folder with content specifically used in the new-sub welcome sequence; (3) a general 'PPV' folder; (4) optionally an 'SFW' folder for feed reposts. When a chatter replays a script with a new subscriber they navigate to the matching script folder to pull the correct assets in order.

variants.claim: Chatting drives 80%+ of OF revenue; chatters must have scripts with specific photos, videos, and voice notes pre-loaded so they can send PPV content in real time without hunting the vault mid-conversation.
variants.what_they_do: Agency builds a script library where each conversation branch has the relevant media asset already tagged/attached. Chatters follow the script and send the pre-matched content without interruption, maintaining flow and urgency in the conversation.

sources.quote: if you have big sexting scripts...you'll create folders in the vault called like script one script two Etc which is where all the content relating to that script will go
the easier you make it for them the less chance of mistakes there will be

80% or more should come from the chatting the messages and the tips so because that's the most important part you must have a system in place so you must have set scripts that you or if you've hired somebody to the to do the chatting they can follow now Within These scripts they should have pre-made content already there so photos and videos maybe even some voice notes

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV content system is THIN (§2) and chatting is currently outsourced (§8). There is no documented vault folder convention or script-to-asset mapping. This is directly relevant to Palm's roadmap item of building an in-house chat team and AI chatting system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As build-toward for the in-house chat team: design the OF vault structure around scripts rather than raw chronology. Create a folder schema (New Subscriber, Script_01…Script_N, PPV_unlabelled, SFW_Feed) before handing off to chatters. Document which assets belong to which script in Airtable so the AI chatting system can programmatically select the right media during a conversation.

findings.title: Configure an auto-sent paid intro message ($15–25) to fire on every new subscriber

variants.claim: Setting up an automated PPV intro message that triggers the moment a fan subscribes generates passive revenue from every new sub with zero chatter effort.
variants.what_they_do: Inside OF settings, a welcome message is pre-loaded with a PPV attachment priced at $15–25. It fires automatically to every new subscriber. No chatter action required. This is the first monetization touchpoint before any live chat begins, and it also flags whether the new sub is a likely spender (purchase = qualified lead for the upsell ladder).

variants.claim: An automated message sent to every new subscriber that creates a conversational scenario and pitches a first video purchase converts cold new fans into paying customers before a live chatter has to engage.
variants.what_they_do: A pre-written automated script goes to every new sub immediately after they subscribe. It creates a light interactive scenario (not specified in detail), leads the fan through a short interaction, and closes with a $34.99 video offer. Once the fan buys, they are flagged as 'warmed up' and handed to regular chat flow at full price floors.

sources.quote: another way that you can quickly gauge how interested a fan is and your content is if they purchase your intro message this is a message that you set up to automatically send out once they hit subscribe
usually our welcome messages are like 15 to 25 bucks but then the first ppv we send is like 5 to 15
it will send to the sub it will create a little scenario it will get them to do something and then it will sell them a video for $34.99

palm_comparison.vs_us: No Palm-documented equivalent (§8 outsourced, §7 GAP on PPV setup). This is a zero-marginal-effort revenue lever that the external chat team can implement immediately for every Palm creator, and should be baked into the in-house chat onboarding checklist.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately brief the external chat team to verify every Palm creator has an auto-send intro PPV configured at $15–25. Make this a checklist item in creator onboarding (§6 form wizard). Track intro-PPV purchase rate per creator as a leading indicator of subscriber quality.

findings.title: Weekly async Loom chat reviews focused on new subscribers + high-ticket purchases

variants.claim: Regular async video reviews (via Loom) of specific conversation types — new subscribers, purchases, high-ticket sales — plus chatter-submitted chats drive measurable skill improvement.
variants.what_they_do: Manager opens the 'new subscribers' tab and reviews those conversations each week. Also reviews purchase logs and high-ticket sales. Asks each chatter to submit specific chats for review. Records Loom feedback videos. Per-review focus stays on one improvement point. Maintains 'scientist mode' (critical, constructive) not 'dictator mode' (anger-driven). Each chatter gets one weekly improvement point plus actionable steps.

variants.claim: Systematically monitoring which specific messages generate the most engagement and sales, then sharing winners as team templates, compounds chatter performance over time.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to set daily sales targets, track which message styles and scripts convert best, and collaborate with teammates to share high-performing templates. If a flirty line is working across multiple fans, it gets codified as a reusable template for the whole team.

sources.quote: We open up the new subscribers tab and read those conversations. We look at purchases. We look for high ticket sales. And we ask chatters every week to give us specific chats to review
Every week we analyze the one point of improvement for each chatter
if a certain flirty message is working well for multiple subscribers you can use it as a template for future chats

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal review cadence at Palm (§8, outsourced). Partially relevant to the AI chatting project as well. Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a weekly review ritual into the in-house chat SOP from day one: manager records a ≤5-min Loom per chatter, logs the single focus point and action steps in Airtable (add a 'Chatter Reviews' table under the chat team's workspace).

findings.title: Promote top-20% chatters to shift leads who own whale accounts and implement systems first

variants.claim: Top-performing chatters should be elevated to shift leads with higher-value responsibilities — handling whales, receiving best subscribers, implementing new systems, and potentially earning better pay — to incentivize excellence without necessarily moving them to pure management.
variants.what_they_do: Top 20% by leaderboard score become shift leads. Responsibilities: handle all whale interactions, assigned the highest-value subscribers, first cohort to test and implement any new system or script, eligible for increased pay tied to sustained performance. They remain chatters but are the internal standard-setters.

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

sources.quote: the top 20%, so the best of the best, they become shift leads. They have the responsibility to handle whales
every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter structure exists at Palm (§8). This shift-lead model is a concrete org design to adopt when building the in-house team — it creates a promotion path without pulling best chatters away from revenue-generating work.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design the in-house chat org with two tiers from the start: chatters and shift leads. Write the shift-lead job description now (whale ownership, system rollout, quality oversight) so the role is clear before the first hire. Tie shift-lead pay to whale revenue metrics, not just tenure.

findings.title: Agency founder must chatter personally before hiring chatters

variants.claim: Before delegating DM monetization to hired chatters, the agency operator must handle messaging themselves to understand fan psychology and what drives spending.
variants.what_they_do: Requires the founder to personally reply to DMs in the early stage — not as a permanent task but as a learning phase. The stated goal is to internalize why fans message, why they send money, and what emotional triggers drive PPV conversions, before writing SOPs or hiring people to do it.

variants.claim: Agency owners should personally do DM chatting for two weeks to understand fan psychology and upsell mechanics before hiring chatters, and should prioritize South African hires over Southeast Asian for native English slang and cultural alignment.
variants.what_they_do: New agency operators personally handle all DMs for roughly two weeks to learn what works. They then hire chatters from South Africa (preferred) or South America, paying in local currency (favorable exchange rate). Three chatters per creator account rotate 8-hour shifts for 24/7 coverage. Chatters are trained by the operator who now understands the product firsthand.

variants.claim: Founders who chat themselves get short-term quality gains but sacrifice the bandwidth needed to get results, attract better creators, and scale.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly stops chatting himself and redirects that time into training chatters to surpass his own skill level. He frames chatter training as a higher-leverage use of founder time than doing the chatting, because it unblocks bandwidth for content direction, acquisition, and results that attract higher-tier creators.

sources.quote: You need to understand the psychology. Why is this dude, you know, typing a message this girl online? Why is he here? Why is he sending money? You need to understand these things.
step two, is to hire chatters... the vast majority of our team is from SA, from South Africa. Mainly because English is their first language
if you are chatting yourself, you're not going to have that bandwidth — the key there is to train your chatters to become better than you

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources all chatting to an external team (§8) and has no internal SOPs, scripts, or fan psychology documentation. This means Palm's leadership may lack the ground-level insight needed to evaluate chatter quality or build in-house scripts for the roadmap in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm moves toward an in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap, §8), leadership should run a time-boxed hands-on chatting sprint (e.g., 2-4 weeks on 1-2 accounts) to build firsthand understanding of fan psychology before writing scripts or hiring. Document findings as the foundation of Palm's internal chatting SOP.

findings.topic: Emotional experience framing vs. transactional selling
findings.title: Sell the intimate experience, not the content — fans pay for emotion, not pornography

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality
the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal script or framing doctrine. This 'experience over transaction' principle should anchor chatter training philosophy when Palm builds in-house. Also directly applicable to AI chatting — arguably more so, since AI chatters must compensate for lack of a real person.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'sell the experience, not the content' the #1 principle in the chatter training SOP. Require chatters to spend a defined minimum number of exchanges on warmup/small talk before any PPV offer. For the AI chatting project, build emotional escalation cues into the conversation state machine — track where in the 'intimacy arc' each fan is before triggering a sell.

findings.title: Use passive teaser content (towel/shower clips) to make the fan initiate sexual escalation, not the chatter

variants.claim: Chatters should never directly initiate sexual conversation — instead, send an ambiguous teaser that makes sexual escalation the fan's idea, reducing friction and preserving the illusion of authenticity.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends a low-reveal teaser (e.g., model in a towel with wet hair, caption: 'just got out of the shower') with no sexual framing. The fan reads the signal and responds sexually — he has technically initiated. Alternatively, a 3-second clip of the model waving to camera with a self-deprecating caption ('my face looks so bad right now') achieves the same passive initiation. Once the fan escalates, the chatter follows his lead and layers in a second, slightly more revealing teaser before setting up the first PPV.

variants.claim: Posting a simple 'I'm online / bored' caption on the creator's feed draws fans into DMs on their own initiative, giving the chatter frame control and avoiding the 'spammy' feel of mass PPV messages.
variants.what_they_do: Instead of mass-sending PPV blasts, the chatter (or creator post) publishes a low-key feed post ('hey I'm up, who's up?', 'come to my DMs right now') that signals availability without directly soliciting money. Fans who respond have self-selected as engaged; the chatter is now in a reactive, high-authority position to run the upsell sequence.

sources.quote: the most important part here is to let him initiate a sexual conversation
post on your page say hey I'm online hey I'm bored um just post a picture caption it and they'll get the idea that you're online
you don't want to ever spam with ppv this is a huge No-No it cheapens your brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8 outsourced). This is a high-value build-toward mechanic — it maps directly to the psychological structure Palm will need when scripting AI chatting (active roadmap project, §8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: In the in-house/AI chat SOP, codify 'fan-initiated escalation' as a required step between rapport and first PPV. Build a library of passive teaser content categories (shower, waving, casual home setting) and matching neutral captions. For AI chatting specifically, this mechanic is low-risk and high-leverage — the AI never has to make a sexual first move.

findings.title: Package VA chatter training as a zipped module with persona PDF

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

sources.quote: some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module
We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting to an external team (§8) with no documented internal SOP or onboarding material. There is no equivalent persona brief or chatter training kit. This is a direct gap to fill as Palm builds toward an in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), create a per-creator ZIP/Notion training module template: persona card (age, niche, tone, emojis, pet names), platform walkthrough, PPV pricing ladder, and a baseline script. Standardize this as the chatter onboarding artifact.

findings.title: Three chatters + reliever for seamless 24/7 shifts

variants.claim: Per-creator chatting is staffed with three eight-hour chatters plus one reliever, with documented handoffs for seamless 24/7 coverage.
variants.what_they_do: Runs 3 chatters (six days) + 1 reliever (three days), all 8-hour shifts; Discord clock-in/out, end-of-shift reports, and at-next-chatter handoff messages so in-progress conversations transfer without gaps.

variants.claim: Without fully standardized scripts across shifts, handoffs mid-selling-sequence cause subscribers to disengage, and the agency loses $1–2K per day specifically at shift-change moments.
variants.what_they_do: Every script, objection-handling sequence, and selling approach is written down and standardized across all shifts. The standard is specific enough that if Chatter A stops mid-sentence, Chatter B can pick up the exact conversation thread without the subscriber noticing a change. Revenue tracking consistently shows the lowest revenue window is at shift change — this is the explicit problem they are solving.

sources.quote: Three chatters and one reliever
standardize the scripts, standardize the objection handling, the selling ways. So if one chatter stops texting mid-sentence, the other chatter can jump in flawlessly
it's always the lowest revenue right when the shift shifts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting and has no internal shift model (§8). This is a concrete staffing template for the future in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 3+1 eight-hour shift model with Discord clock-in/EOS reports as the staffing blueprint when Palm builds its in-house chat team.

findings.title: Google Form onboarding captures creator profile, device specs, goals, and personal traits

variants.claim: A structured onboarding form (Google Forms) that captures age, location, device model, personal traits, and goals is the operational foundation before any work begins.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sends a Google Form at signing. Fields include: age, country, iPhone model (iPhone 13+ required for IG quality standard), personal traits, content style preferences, and creator goals. Login to OF account follows, then profile audit, promotions review, and a written growth plan.

variants.claim: A structured onboarding folder with three components — model info sheet, platform logins, and pre-made PPV content scripts — prevents miscommunication and enables the chatting team to start immediately.
variants.what_they_do: On signing, the agency shares a Google Drive folder. The model info sheet captures personal details (favorite color, food, pet names, etc.) that chatters need to maintain consistent persona in DMs. Logins for all managed platforms are collected at once. Pre-made scripts (photo/video sequences already shot) are also uploaded so chatters can sell PPV from day one. Expectations are set in writing: communication only via WhatsApp group, Monday–Friday 9–5 local time.

sources.quote: get up a Google Forms, fill it out — you want to know when, how old the model is, where the model's from, if the model's got an iPhone, what iPhone she's recording off
we always do iPhone 13 or above, they have to be, because Instagram care a lot about quality
within our Google Drive folder, we have three things. the model information sheets... the login... and then last but not least is the scripts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has an onboarding form wizard (name, email, commission %, state, contract, voice memo — §6) but no documented device requirement, content style intake, or goal-setting fields. The iPhone 13+ quality gate is not in Palm's onboarding.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's onboarding wizard to include: (1) device model field with a minimum spec gate (iPhone 13+ or Android equivalent), (2) content style/persona fields, (3) creator goals (revenue target, posting comfort level, explicit limits). Add an OF profile audit step as a formal onboarding milestone before first post.

findings.title: Run 3-variant script tests with unlock-rate tracking before standardising any chatting SOP

variants.claim: Running three concurrent script variants with measured unlock rates — rather than just deploying a new script agency-wide — reveals the specific element driving performance and prevents agencies from unknowingly using inferior scripts.
variants.what_they_do: When a new chatting script is introduced, the agency assigns variant A, B, and C to different chatters or conversation batches, tracks PPV unlock rate per variant, diagnoses why the winning variant beat the others (e.g., opening line framing), and only then canonises the best approach across all chatters.

variants.claim: The most effective chat scripts are built bottom-up from live testing — chatters freestyle, track what gets positive responses and high PPV buy rates, and promote those lines/setups into the official script iteratively.
variants.what_they_do: The agency does not hand chatters a finished script to copy-paste. They provide the structural framework (rapport → passive tease → yes train → PPV ladder → gratification close) and then chatters freestyle within it. When a specific line or PPV setup consistently produces positive fan responses or high buy rates, it gets promoted into the agency's canonical script. The script is therefore a living document refined by actual conversion data.

sources.quote: test every single one of them. Okay, this script got a 50% unlock rate. The other variation is 30%, this is 55. Okay, why?
If we see that a certain sentence has positive feedback from most subscribers, we will use it in the script. If a PPV has a high buy rate with a certain PPV setup, we will use it in the script
how we create scripts in our agency is we get on the page, we start chatting and see what works

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is currently outsourced at Palm (§8 — OUTSOURCED); no internal script management or test infrastructure exists today. This is a build-toward finding for the planned in-house and AI chatting projects.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When designing Palm's in-house chat SOP (§8 roadmap), build variant testing into the process architecture from day one: store script variants in Airtable with a linked 'test result' table capturing unlock rate and revenue per test run. Do not import a competitor's script wholesale — always test 3 variants first.

findings.title: Per-source tracking links expose where revenue originates

variants.claim: Operators run the funnel as a tracked numbers game: per-platform tracking links reveal where subs and revenue actually come from and surface conversion bottlenecks.
variants.what_they_do: A separate Fanvue tracking link per social account measures clicks, subs, revenue and per-source CTR; a low CTR (under 1-2%) flags a broken bio/CTA, X funnel or Fanvue setup to fix; spend-per-sub is calculated per source to decide where to invest time and money.

variants.claim: Using a dedicated tracking link for each traffic source (e.g., one link for Twitter) lets you measure exactly how much OF revenue each platform generates.
variants.what_they_do: Each traffic channel (X account, IG, promo page, etc.) gets its own tracking/inflow link. Griffin shows a single Twitter tracking link that generated $186K in OF revenue, proving channel-level attribution. This enables decisions like 'Twitter is responsible for ~40–50% of this creator's revenue growth' rather than guessing.

sources.quote: a direct tracking link created on fan view for X only
A good CTR is 3 to 5%. Less than 1 to 2% means your funnel's weak
This is a tracking link in inflow from one of our Twitter accounts on our creator using this strategy and we've generated $186,000 through this link.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm tracks invoicing/data (§7, §9) and has Fan Tracker, but cross-channel sub attribution and funnel-leak diagnostics are not documented; traffic measurement is a gap (§5).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement per-source tracking links plus a per-channel CTR / spend-per-sub view for real creators to attribute OF subs to their social source and diagnose funnel leaks.

findings.title: Weekly cadence and standards prevent regression to chaos

variants.claim: A fixed weekly operating cadence plus enforced standards (recognition and consequences) keeps an agency from regressing into reactive firefighting.
variants.what_they_do: Installs a Monday hour-one reset (look back, look forward, pick 1-3 priorities, schedule, overflow), a weekly single 'game tape' root-cause review, decision-only compressed 30-min meetings, and a recognition/consequence system; 'culture is what you tolerate and what you reinforce.'

variants.claim: Weekly payouts ('weekly pouch') keep both the agency and creator motivated and signal operational professionalism early in the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After onboarding, agency moves to a weekly payout cadence. Framed as both a financial rhythm and a morale tool — seeing money arrive weekly reinforces creator buy-in and agency credibility. No detailed reporting format described, but the weekly cadence is presented as standard practice.

sources.quote: Without rhythm, everything will regress into chaos
Culture is what you tolerate and culture is what you reinforce
after a week or two, we do weekly pouch, just to let you know — that's just a cool little tip, enjoy the money coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm coordinates via Telegram with no documented operating cadence, scorecards, or standards-enforcement rhythm (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Install a lightweight weekly cadence (priorities reset + one root-cause review) and a recognition/consequence standard across Palm's team and contractors.

findings.title: Replicate local folder structure 1-to-1 on Google Drive as business-continuity backup

variants.claim: If the agency's local machine were destroyed, the business should still be able to operate — so every folder and file that is critical to operations is mirrored on Google Drive in the exact same structure.
variants.what_they_do: The agency maintains a Google Drive that mirrors the local computer: a top-level 'Raw' folder per creator containing sub-folders for raw content, photo grid, invoices, promotional assets, OF stories, and the services agreement (contract). The Raw folder is shared directly with the model (right-click → share access → pink highlight convention so the manager knows it is externally shared) so models can self-upload content.

variants.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.

sources.quote: if my laptop dropped or fell in a pool or caught on fire or lightning hit it or a car ran it over could the business still operate if the answer is no then I back everything up to the Google Drive
the Royal folder pink that means that this folder has been shared with the model
the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses Airtable + Cloudflare media for asset storage (§1) and Dropbox for invoice delivery (§7). A formal business-continuity / disaster-recovery policy for media assets is not documented. The model self-upload workflow exists (creators upload to become Assets) but the specific shared-folder colour-coding convention and the 'can the business function without this laptop' checklist mindset are not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a formal backup policy: define which data stores are business-critical (Airtable, Cloudflare media, contract PDFs, invoices) and ensure at least one redundant copy exists outside the primary system. For creator onboarding, consider a shared Google Drive or Cloudflare folder (colour/tag-coded as 'creator-accessible') as the upload landing zone, mirroring the model-upload convenience described here.

findings.title: Commission-plus-bonus comp structure with daily ranked leaderboard drives consistent chatter output

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

variants.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
variants.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.

sources.quote: every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result
media team are compensated for these videos doing well. Marketing team are compensated for more traffic. Chat team are compensated for more sales.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented chatter hiring criteria, comp structure, or performance management cadence (§8 outsourced, §10 THIN). This is the operational skeleton Palm will need when building the in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When scoping the in-house chat team build (~1yr roadmap per §8): (1) Design a commission + milestone-bonus comp structure before the first hire — do not default to hourly. (2) Implement a daily revenue leaderboard visible to the whole chat team. (3) Build a pre-training filter (written test + role-play) that candidates must pass before formal onboarding. (4) Schedule weekly chat reviews for each chatter, not just when numbers drop. These four structural elements can be planned now and should be in the in-house team design spec.

findings.title: Hire for coachability and reliability, not pre-existing chatting skill

variants.claim: The correct chatter hiring filter is competence, reliability, and honesty — not existing chatting skill — because a motivated person can be trained to top-level in 2–3 weeks.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly de-emphasize prior chatting experience as a hiring criterion. Instead prioritize candidates who demonstrate effort, follow-through, and personal motivation throughout the screening pipeline. Agency trains chatters to top level within 2–3 weeks once the right person is selected. The entire multi-stage funnel (attention checks, situational tests, live calls, Loom compliance) is designed to surface coachable, reliable people — not to find pre-trained chatters.

variants.claim: The cost-effective chatting model is hiring low-cost offshore chatters and de-risking them through heavy recurring training plus fully systemized scripts so output doesn't depend on their prior experience.
variants.what_they_do: Hires Filipino chatters at ~$2-3/hour via OnlineJobs.ph, distrusts 'experienced' chatters who spam PPVs, and trains them personally 4-5 times a week (~20-30 min) on his own systems so every chatter sounds identical and 'can't mess up'.

sources.quote: It's about you hiring competent, reliable, and honest people that you can teach the skill of chatting. Because we taught chatters in like two or three weeks to get to a top level.
Go to online jobs.ph. Get yourself a Filipino chatter. Two $3 per hour
teaching your chatters at least three times per week for 30 minutes a day minimum

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter hiring criteria (§8 outsourced). This philosophy should anchor Palm's future hiring rubric — it means Palm can source from a much wider talent pool and doesn't need to pay a premium for 'experienced' chatters.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document 'coachability, reliability, honesty' as Palm's three core chatter hiring criteria. Build the scoring rubric for each screening stage around these three traits rather than chatting knowledge. This also reduces cost — experienced chatters command higher pay; trainable reliable people do not.

findings.title: Source chatters from onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, OFM Jobs — never Telegram

variants.claim: The three reliable hiring platforms for chatters are onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs; Telegram chatters are consistently low quality.
variants.what_they_do: Post job listings on onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs. Explicitly avoid sourcing from Telegram groups. Also mentions some Facebook groups and other Filipino hiring sites as secondary options.

variants.claim: The cost-effective chatting model is hiring low-cost offshore chatters and de-risking them through heavy recurring training plus fully systemized scripts so output doesn't depend on their prior experience.
variants.what_they_do: Hires Filipino chatters at ~$2-3/hour via OnlineJobs.ph, distrusts 'experienced' chatters who spam PPVs, and trains them personally 4-5 times a week (~20-30 min) on his own systems so every chatter sounds identical and 'can't mess up'.

sources.quote: post a job post on onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs. Those are like the three main hiring sites. Don't get them off of Telegram.
Go to online jobs.ph. Get yourself a Filipino chatter. Two $3 per hour
teaching your chatters at least three times per week for 30 minutes a day minimum

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced to an external team (§8); no internal hiring process exists yet. When Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr out), it has no documented sourcing channels. This is a direct gap to fill.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm begins building its in-house chat team, establish onlinejobs.ph as the primary sourcing channel. Create a blacklist policy against Telegram-sourced chatters. Document this as the first step in the future chatter hiring SOP.

findings.topic: Minimum viable team structure for one creator
findings.title: Three core hires outside chatting: account manager, SMM, and model-facing manager

variants.claim: An agency running even one creator needs three non-chatter roles: an account manager (OF vault/feed), a social media manager (IG/Reddit/Twitter), and a model-facing manager who relays communication between backend and creator.
variants.what_they_do: Account manager owns the OF side; social media manager owns all platform posting and content direction; a 'model manager' (ideally first-world, trusted) sits between both and the creator — giving her content lists, flagging trending sounds, celebrating milestones, and fielding her daily questions. This person acts as the Swiss-army-knife ops lead who reports to the founder daily on whether Reddit posts went out, tweets sent, IG up, etc. Founder only sets direction, not execution.

variants.claim: Free the owner by splitting the agency into core departments, each run by a manager the owner reports through, instead of doing everything personally.
variants.what_they_do: Typically three departments — client acquisition, marketing, chatting — each with a manager (e.g. chatter manager, marketing director, acquisition manager). The owner checks in with managers (not every IC) once each morning; managers run the ICs (outreach VAs, editors, chatters). Beginners can start with 3-6 chatters + one chatter manager. Trying to do it all solo makes scaling to $1M/mo 'physically impossible.'

sources.quote: account manager to just oversee the only fans account then you would have a social media manager to oversee everything that comes between all socials whether it be Instagram Tik Tok Reddit Twitter
you want to have three four main departments... Client acquisition, marketing, and chatting... have a manager in client acquisition, a manager in marketing, and a manager in chatting.
once in the morning, you check in with all of the managers.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org (§10) is thin: Josh runs acquisition/strategy, Evan runs tech/ops, Amin handles manual posting, editors handle video, and an external chat team covers DMs. There is no documented model-facing manager role, no SMM-layer employee, and no internal account manager layer. Communication between backend ops and creators runs through Josh/Evan directly, which doesn't scale. Palm's SMM consolidation (§11) is a tech fix; this is the human-layer equivalent that Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and hire (or designate internally) the three-role stack as Palm scales past 3-4 creators: (1) OF account manager per cluster of creators, (2) SMM coordinator owning platform execution, (3) model-facing manager as creator relationship layer. The model manager role in particular should be a priority hire before the next creator signing — it directly reduces founder bottleneck and improves creator retention (§9 gap).

findings.topic: Reddit and X as supplementary traffic channels
findings.title: Contract out Reddit and X content distribution as a low-overhead traffic stream

variants.claim: Outsourcing Reddit and X content distribution to a specialist contractor can yield disproportionate revenue relative to cost (cited: $1K/month spend generating ~$20K/month in revenue).
variants.what_they_do: Agency pays a Reddit/X specialist contractor roughly $1,000/month to distribute content across relevant subreddits and X accounts, funneling traffic to OF. The contractor is found through the agency's Telegram network. ROI is tracked as revenue attributed to that traffic source versus contractor cost.

variants.claim: Agencies still spending on Reddit, paid ads, SFS, and GG swaps are watching already-thin margins deteriorate and should reallocate entirely to organic social.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant explicitly names Reddit spend, paid advertising, SFS (shoutout-for-shoutout), and GG (girl-girl) swaps as legacy tactics that are now net-negative when factoring in declining ROI and margin compression, advocating a full pivot to organic social content.

sources.quote: I pay him like a thousand bucks a month and he brings me like $20,000 a month
your Reddit and X contractors are distributing content and funneling into loads of subs turning into hefty profit
Many agencies are still using the old methods, throwing money at Reddit and paid ads and SFS and GG swaps, and their already razor thin margins are getting worse by the day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit or X traffic strategy (§5 GAP). This is a pure learn-from-others gap that applies to real creators and is relatively easy to test at low cost.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Source a Reddit/X contractor via OFM Telegram groups. Start with a 30-day paid pilot (~$500–$1K) for one creator, using UTM-tagged links or OF referral tracking to attribute revenue. If 5x+ ROI is confirmed, formalize as a recurring line item per creator.

findings.title: Threads 30-slave-account follow-bot: 1,200 targeted leads/day via viral-post follower targeting

variants.claim: Running 30+ Threads slave accounts that each follow 40–50 recent likers of viral similar posts generates ~1,200 targeted daily leads who see the main account funnel.
variants.what_they_do: One main Threads account posts daily content with Fanvue link. 30–50 slave accounts each have bio text 'I'm more active here' tagging the main. A custom Chrome extension bot finds recently viral posts in the niche, filters likes by recency, and auto-follows those users (40–50 per slave per session). Notified users see a profile → visit main → subscribe. AdsPower browser manages all slave accounts. The bot is distributed via private Discord.

variants.claim: A single creator can be scaled across up to 15–20 Threads accounts with near-linear compounding returns if content and operational capacity allow.
variants.what_they_do: TDM runs multiple Threads accounts per creator (up to 15–20), treating content volume and staff/hardware as the only real constraints. They use a phone farm for IG as the mechanism for managing Threads accounts and explicitly avoid automation tools (schedulers/outlers) due to Meta's sensitivity, preferring manual or native posting.

sources.quote: You need 30 50 of these. They just have to post and in the bar you can say I'm more active here and tag your main account
30 slave accounts, multiply by 40 follows, 1,200 targeted leads a day, you will get subscribers

if you've got enough content and the content performs and you see return on investment, you could run up to 15 20 accounts per creator. The bottleneck here is often times the manpower to actually run that and the hardware

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Threads strategy or follower-growth automation (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm does use Publer for scheduling but not engagement automation. This level of account farming on Threads is outside Palm's current documented ops.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona scaling line (§3), evaluate the Threads slave-account follow strategy as a traffic lever. The math (1,200 leads/day) is compelling if sustainable. Before adopting: assess Threads TOS risk (similar to IG follow-loop risks) and whether AdsPower fits alongside existing GrapheneOS/hardware isolation setup (§4). Start with a small 5-account pilot before scaling to 30+.

findings.topic: X content rules — tease vs. reveal
findings.title: No Full Nudity on X — Use Tease-Only Content to Pull Higher-Spending Fans

variants.claim: Posting full nudity on X attracts low-spending free-content seekers; tease-only premium content attracts higher-demographic buyers who will pay on OF.
variants.what_they_do: Main account posts only professional photos, good angles, clean aesthetic — no full nudity. Full explicit content is reserved exclusively for the OF vault. The X page functions purely as top-of-funnel tease. No OF link on burner accounts — only on the main. Strategy is: build personality + tease body, never reveal. Explicit quote: 'Do tease, but don't reveal. People follow people, not just bodies.'

variants.claim: Twitter allows near-explicit content, making it a materially higher-virality channel for OF funnels than IG/TikTok — agencies should set it up at onboarding and teach creators to reply to viral tweets.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sets up Twitter for any creator who lacks it during onboarding, teaches creator to post more explicit (but compliant) content and to reply to trending/viral tweets to capture organic reach. Frames Twitter as a separate traffic channel from IG/TikTok, not a duplicate.

sources.quote: Do not post full nudity. Save that for OF. Do tease, but don't reveal.
If you post childish content or you post full nude content, you will pull lower spending fans.
on Twitter you can get away with more not safeer workor content so AKA showing everything and the chance of that going viral is a lot higher and Twitter's full of that right

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault content is a documented GAP (§2 — thin). This finding reinforces a content tiering principle: social = tease, OF = explicit. Palm's social pipeline (§1) produces top-of-funnel content but has no explicit rule documented about nudity levels per platform. This is a rule Palm should codify.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a content-tier rule to Palm's SOP: X/IG/TikTok = tease only (no nudity, professional quality). OF vault = exclusive explicit. Document this as a creator briefing item in onboarding and in the editor task spec so editors never post full nudity to social channels.

findings.title: Snapchat + read-only Telegram channel as a ban-resilient backup funnel

variants.claim: Operators add low-effort Snapchat reach and an owned Telegram broadcast channel to nurture leads and survive social bans, since owned audiences persist when accounts die.
variants.what_they_do: ~15 min/day Snapchat: public profile, repurposed 9:16 content, 2-3 stories/day, Telegram CTA only once/week at peak time, no DMs/links to avoid bans; funnels into a read-only Telegram broadcast channel (pinned Fanvue link, niche real-life teasing, reaction-bait) that both converts and acts as a 'safe haven' to relaunch socials after a ban.

variants.claim: Snapchat is an under-exploited OFM channel that simultaneously drives OF subs, cross-promotes other platforms, and pays direct creator monetization.
